If parts are beyond repair, it might be better to replace the asset than repair
it. In this way, the daily operations continue to run at maximum efficiency.
Most tangible assets, such as vehicles, machines, and production equipment, go
through a maintenance and repair journey. However, intangible assets, such as
software, brands, intellectual property (IP), patents, and so on, might also
require maintenance and repair. Investment in intangible assets is expensed
instead of capitalized.
The owner of an asset can lease it to another party for money or another factor.
Maintenance and repair of leased assets might depend on the agreement between
the lessor and the lessee, based on whether the lease is a capital lease or an
operating lease.

l3Processes.title: Forecast maintenance
l3Processes.description:
Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps enables users to forecast maintenance
needs based on asset usage, historical data, and predictive analytics.
Maintenance forecasts help plan resource allocation, budget for repairs, and
optimize asset uptime, leveraging system-generated insights and historical
trends.

l3Processes.title: Schedule maintenance jobs
l3Processes.description:
Users can schedule maintenance jobs for assets, assigning technicians,
resources, and time slots. The system supports automated scheduling based on
maintenance plans, asset conditions, and resource availability, with
notifications and work order management.

l3Processes.title: Perform preventative maintenance
l3Processes.description:
Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps supports execution of preventative
maintenance tasks according to defined schedules. Users can record completed
maintenance, update asset status, and track compliance with maintenance plans.
Preventative maintenance helps extend asset life and reduce unplanned downtime.

l3Processes.title: Perform corrective maintenance
l3Processes.description:
Users can initiate corrective maintenance for assets when failures or issues are
detected. The system supports work order creation, technician assignment, parts
requisition, and recording of repair activities. Corrective maintenance is
tracked for cost analysis and asset reliability.

l3Processes.title: Perform predictive maintenance
l3Processes.description:
Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps supports predictive maintenance by
analyzing asset data, sensor inputs, and historical trends to anticipate
failures and schedule maintenance before breakdowns occur. The system integrates
with IoT devices and analytics platforms for advanced maintenance planning.

l3Processes.title: Scrap an asset

apqc.description: Process and record fixed-asset adjustments, enhancements, revaluations, and transfers

l3Processes.description:
In the rapidly evolving business environment of today, for any organization, the
process of retiring and disposing of assets holds a pivotal role in the
lifecycle management of resources within that organization. The retire and
dispose of assets business process involves retiring and disposing of fixed
assets that are no longer needed. It also covers when assets must be replaced by
assessing the lifecycle stage, usability, and alignment of assets with the
organizational goals.
As assets mature, outgrow their usefulness, or no longer fit the evolving
demands of the business, or as technology evolves, the strategic choice to
retire and dispose of assets is crucial. Making informed decisions about their
retirement and disposal ensures that the organization maintains operational
efficiency, optimizes resource allocation, and adheres to legal and
environmental responsibilities.
When a fixed asset is no longer needed, it can either be scrapped or sold. If
the asset still has value, it's likely to be set to be sold. When a fixed asset
is sold with profit, the profit is recognized in the year of the sale, and then
it's posted to the profit and loss part of the chart of accounts.

l3Processes.title: Sell an asset
l3Processes.description:
Selling an asset in Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps involves disposing
of an asset through a sale transaction. The process includes marking the asset
as available for sale, stopping depreciation, recording the sale proceeds,
posting the necessary accounting entries to remove the asset from the books, and
updating the asset’s status. The system supports tracking the sale process,
linking to customer records, and generating audit trails for compliance.

l3Processes.title: Analyze fixed assets
l3Processes.description:
This process involves reviewing the fixed asset register, including acquisition,
depreciation, impairment, and disposal activities. Dynamics 365 finance and
operations apps provides comprehensive reporting and analysis tools to monitor
asset lifecycles, track book values, and support audit and compliance
requirements.

l3Processes.title: Replace an asset

apqc.description: Process and record fixed-asset adjustments, enhancements, revaluations, and transfers

l3Processes.description:
Replacing an asset in Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps involves retiring
an existing asset and acquiring a new one, often as part of a planned
replacement cycle. The process includes identifying the asset for replacement,
initiating the disposal (sale, scrap, or retirement), updating asset records,
and linking the new asset acquisition to the replacement event. The system
supports tracking replacement history, financial impacts, and compliance with
capitalization policies.

l3Processes.title: Retire assets

apqc.description: Process and record fixed-asset additions and retires

l3Processes.description:
Retiring an asset in Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps involves removing
an asset from active use and the fixed asset register, typically when it is
fully depreciated or no longer needed. The process includes initiating a
retirement transaction, updating the asset’s status, posting the necessary
accounting entries, and archiving the asset record. The system maintains an
audit trail and supports compliance with accounting policies.

l3Processes.title: Decommission assets

apqc.description: Process and record fixed-asset additions and retires

l3Processes.description:
Decommissioning an asset in Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps involves
formally removing an asset from service, often as a precursor to scrapping,
selling, or retiring it. The process includes updating the asset’s operational
status, initiating the appropriate disposal transaction, and ensuring all
financial and compliance requirements are met. The system tracks decommissioned
assets and supports audit and compliance needs.

l3Processes.title: Monitor asset inventory
l3Processes.description:
This process involves reviewing and reporting on the current inventory of assets
and their associated valuations. Dynamics 365 Finance & Operations provides
tools to track asset acquisition costs, accumulated depreciation, book values,
and asset locations. Users can generate reports to assess the completeness and
accuracy of asset records, monitor changes in asset values, and support
financial reporting and compliance.

l3Processes.title: Analyze asset leases
l3Processes.description:
This process entails reviewing lease agreements, tracking lease liabilities and
right-of-use assets, and analyzing lease-related expenses and commitments.
Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps supports lease accounting in compliance
with relevant standards, providing reports on lease balances, schedules, and
financial impacts.

l3Processes.title: Monitor asset performance
l3Processes.description:
Monitoring asset performance involves tracking key metrics such as uptime,
downtime, maintenance frequency, and operational efficiency. Dynamics 365
finance and operations apps can be configured to capture performance data,
generate alerts, and provide dashboards for asset managers to assess asset
health and optimize utilization.

l3Processes.title: Analyze maintenance costs
l3Processes.description:
This process focuses on capturing, categorizing, and analyzing maintenance
expenses associated with assets. Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps
supports tracking maintenance costs by asset, cost center, or project, enabling
detailed cost analysis and supporting decisions on asset replacement or
retention.

l3Processes.title: Analyze asset utilization
l3Processes.description:
Analyzing asset utilization involves measuring how effectively assets are used,
including tracking usage hours, mileage, and assignment to projects or cost
centers. Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps can support utilization
analysis through integration with operational systems and by providing reports
that inform asset allocation and replacement strategies.

l3Processes.title: Reconcile asset subledger
l3Processes.description:
Reconciling the asset subledger involves ensuring that the fixed asset subledger
in Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps matches the general ledger and other
operational systems. The process includes identifying discrepancies,
investigating root causes, and posting adjustments as needed to maintain
financial integrity.

l3Processes.title: Create knowledge base articles

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description:
As a consultant, I need to understand the process/details to create, revise, and publish knowledge base articles so that internally or externally knowledge can be searched and used to resolve issues before or after a case is opened. 
 The AI system/copilot will walk me through the steps required to create my first knowledge base article. Ask the configuration prerequisites (Lookups, values, or other supporting entities) The expectation is to provide an order of prerequisites (1-x) Provide the user with the ability to fill in the details from each pre-requisite area (Knowledge Articles, queues, skills and SLAs by order) Once complete, I can move on to the next area Once all pre-requisites are entered, I should now be able to create a new knowledge base article (From user perspective)    Security Roles for Creating Knowledge Base Articles in D365 Customer Service In Dynamics 365 Customer Service, users need specific security roles to create and manage knowledge base (KB) articles. The key roles involved in KB management include: Knowledge Manager – Full control over knowledge articles, including publishing and managing content. Knowledge Author – Can create, edit, and submit knowledge articles but does not have publishing permissions. Customer Service Manager – Can manage customer service operations, including knowledge articles. Customer Service Representative – Typically has read-only access to published knowledge articles but may have permissions to create drafts. System Administrator – Has full access, including the ability to configure knowledge management settings.  Steps to Create a Knowledge Base Article in D365 Customer Service Follow these steps to create a KB article in Dynamics 365 Customer Service: Step 1: Navigate to Knowledge Management Sign in to Dynamics 365 Customer Service. In the Customer Service Workspace, select Knowledge Articles from the left navigation pane.  Step 2: Create a New Knowledge Article Click + New to create a new article. Fill in the required fields:Title – Enter a meaningful title. Content – Add the main content of the article in the rich text editor. Keywords – Add keywords to improve searchability. Description – Provide a summary of the article. Expiration Date – Set if applicable. Subject – Categorize the article under relevant subjects.    Step 3: Add Attachments (Optional) If needed, attach images, documents, or other reference materials.  Step 4: Review and Submit for Approval Click Save to draft the article. Click Submit to send the article for review. If approval is required, a Knowledge Manager will review and approve/reject it.  Step 5: Publish the Article Once approved, click Publish to make the article available to users. Choose the publishing options:Internal – Visible only to employees. External – Available to customers via the self-service portal. Restricted – Limited access based on permissions.

l3Processes.title: Identify common issues

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description:
As a support analyst, I need to understand the process of identifying and documenting common issues so that knowledge article(s) can be created to assist with the resolution. 
Step 1: Analyze Case Data 🔹 Review Historical Cases Navigate to Customer Service Hub → Cases Filter cases by high frequency, repeat issues, or escalations Look at case categories, subcategories, and resolutions  🔹 Use Case Reports & Dashboards Power BI or D365 Dashboards can highlight trends Identify cases with long resolution times (indicating complex issues needing documentation)  🔹 Case Topics & AI Insights If AI is enabled, use Customer Service Insights to detect recurring themes Look at sentiment analysis to find frustrating issues  Step 2: Gather Feedback from Support Agents 🔹 Conduct Agent Interviews & Surveys Ask agents about frequently asked questions Identify issues where customers require repeated clarification  🔹 Review Case Notes & Internal Communication Look for common troubleshooting steps agents take Check for workarounds or undocumented solutions  Step 3: Leverage Automation for Issue Detection 🔹 Use AI & Copilot Suggestions Enable Copilot in D365 Customer Service to suggest common topics Copilot can auto-recommend knowledge articles based on case content  🔹 Case Deflection Metrics If self-service portal usage is low, identify gaps in knowledge Track cases that could have been resolved without human interaction  Step 4: Validate & Prioritize Issues for KB Articles 🔹 Rank Issues Based on Frequency & Impact High-frequency issues should be top priority Complex or time-consuming cases where documentation improves efficiency  🔹 Cross-check with Product/Support Teams Validate solutions with SMEs or product specialists  Step 5: Create & Publish Knowledge Articles 🔹 Use the Knowledge Management Module Title: Clear & concise problem statement Content: Step-by-step resolution Keywords: Ensure searchability Attachments: Screenshots, guides, or videos  🔹 Define Article Visibility Internal (for agents only) External (self-service portal & Copilot)  🔹 Review & Update Regularly Set review reminders for articles to prevent outdated information  Step 6: Monitor & Improve Knowledge Base Usage 🔹 Track KB Article Effectiveness Measure case deflection rates Get feedback from agents & customers  🔹 Update Based on Feedback If agents report missing steps, refine the article If self-service adoption is low, improve structure & searchability

l3Processes.title: Maintain knowledge base articles

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description:
As a Knowledge Manager, I need to know how to review existing articles, identify updates, and provide the updates in knowledge base articles so that they can be reviewed/approved so the updated content is available to support analysts.  
 1. Reviewing Knowledge Base Articles A structured review process ensures that KB articles remain useful and up-to-date. This includes: A. Identifying Articles for Review Use expiration dates and review schedules to trigger a review. Monitor article feedback (user ratings, comments, and usage stats). Use search analytics to determine if articles are effectively addressing customer needs. Assign review responsibility to a Knowledge Manager or a designated team.  B. Evaluating Articles Relevance: Is the article still applicable to current products, services, or processes? Accuracy: Does the content contain outdated information or incorrect details? Clarity: Is the article well-structured, concise, and easy to understand? Format & Compliance: Does it adhere to KB style guidelines and regulatory requirements?  2. Updating Knowledge Base Articles Once an article is identified for update, follow these steps: A. Initiate the Update Process The assigned editor or SME (Subject Matter Expert) creates a new draft version of the article. Changes should align with corporate guidelines, product updates, or new best practices.  B. Editing and Revising Make necessary content updates (text, images, embedded videos, or links). Ensure formatting meets KB standards (structured sections like Overview, Steps, FAQs). Tag the article with relevant categories and keywords for better discoverability.  C. Internal Review by Peers A peer or Knowledge Author verifies the technical accuracy of the changes. The article is checked for grammar, clarity, and formatting consistency.  3. Submitting for Approval Before publishing, the article must go through an approval process: A. Reviewer Assignment A designated reviewer or approver (Knowledge Manager, QA, or Team Lead) is assigned. The reviewer gets notified via D365 workflows or Power Automate.  B. Review Process The reviewer validates content accuracy, completeness, and policy compliance. They provide comments or request changes if needed. If changes are needed, the article is sent back for revision.  C. Approval and Publishing Once approved, the article moves to a published state. It becomes available in the Customer Service portal, internal support interface, or chatbot integration.  4. Ongoing Maintenance and Governance Regular Reviews: Schedule a periodic review cycle (e.g., every 6 months). Feedback Incorporation: Address customer feedback and internal team suggestions. Archival of Old Articles: Remove or retire articles that are no longer relevant.  Workflow in Dynamics 365 Customer Service Identify articles needing updates (scheduled review, feedback, analytics). Edit & Draft the article with necessary updates. Submit for Review to an assigned reviewer. Approve & Publish once the reviewer confirms accuracy. Monitor & Maintain articles using review schedules and feedback.

l3Processes.title: Retire knowledge base articles

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description: As a Knowledge Manager, I want to retire outdated Knowledge Base articles within the Customer Service Workspace so that agents and customers only access the most relevant and accurate information while preserving historical content.  Updated Steps to Retire a Knowledge Base (KB) Article in D365 Customer Service Retiring a Knowledge Base (KB) article in D365 Customer Service Workspace ensures that outdated or incorrect content is no longer accessible while preserving it for historical reference. 1. Steps to Retire a KB Article in Customer Service Workspace Step 1: Navigate to the Knowledge Articles Section Open Customer Service Workspace. In the left navigation pane, select Knowledge Articles.  Step 2: Locate the Article to Retire Use the Search bar or apply Filters to find the article. Click on the Knowledge Article you want to retire.  Step 3: Change the Article Status to "Retired" In the article record, locate the Status field. Click on the Status dropdown and select Retired. Click Save.  Step 4: Validate the Article is No Longer Active Once an article is retired:It will no longer appear in search results for agents and customers. It cannot be linked to cases or suggested in case resolution. It remains stored in the system for historical reference.    Step 5: Archive or Delete (Optional) Archive: Retired articles remain available for internal tracking and compliance. Delete: Administrators can permanently remove articles if they are no longer needed.

l3Processes.title: Define agent skill sets

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.parentL2: Define customer and employee service operations
l3Processes.description: As a user / HR Manager, I need to understand the process of defining an agent skill set, including required skills, gaps, training plans, so that support analysts/agents are assigned to the proper skills to support the areas they are best suited.  Defining Agent Skill Set in D365 Case Management is part of Omnichannel for Customer Service and Unified Routing. It plays a crucial role in intelligent case routing but is not a mandatory prerequisite for basic case creation. However, if you're implementing automated case routing and queue management, agent skill definitions should be configured. Where to Define Agent Skill Sets? Agent skills are primarily set up in the Customer Service Admin Center or Omnichannel for Customer Service under the Work Distribution and Routing settings. Steps to Define Agent Skill Set for Case Routing Enable Unified Routing (If Not Already Enabled) Go to Customer Service Admin Center > Work Distribution > Unified Routing. Enable Unified Routing to allow intelligent case assignments.   Define Skills Navigate to Customer Service Admin Center > Agent Experiences > Skills. Click New Skill and define:Skill Name (e.g., "Warranty Claims Processing"). Skill Category (Optional for grouping). Skill Proficiency Levels (Optional: Beginner, Intermediate, Advanced).     Assign Skills to Agents Go to Customer Service Admin Center > Agent Experiences > Agent Profiles. Select an Agent Profile or create a new one. Assign skills based on expertise.   Configure Skill-Based Routing Rules Navigate to Work Distribution & Routing > Routing Rulesets. Define a new Routing Rule for cases based on attributes like:Case Type Case Subject Customer Priority Entitlement   Assign the rule to a queue with agents who have the required skills.   Assign Agents to Queues Go to Workstreams > Create a Workstream (or modify an existing one). Add Skill Matching Criteria to route cases based on agent skills. Assign Agents to queues based on their defined skills.   Enable Auto-Assignment of Cases to Agents Ensure auto-assignment is enabled within Routing & Work Distribution. Cases will now be assigned to the best-matched agent based on skill proficiency.    How It Works in Case Management? When a case is created, unified routing checks agent availability, assigned skills, and proficiency. The system intelligently routes the case to the best-qualified agent. If no skilled agent is available, fallback rules (such as routing to a general queue) apply.  Where Does This Fit in the Case Creation Process? Agent skill set definition fits under Step 4 (Queues & Routing) in case management setup. While not mandatory for case creation, it is essential for intelligent case assignment. Would you like help configuring this in your D365 environment?

l3Processes.title: Define queues and teams

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.parentL2: Define customer and employee service operations
l3Processes.description: Title: As a Customer Service Manager, I want to understand how to define Teams and Queues within D365 Customer Service so that cases are routed efficiently to the correct team and queue.  Defining Queues and Teams in D365 Customer Service Queues and Teams are essential components in D365 Customer Service for managing and routing cases efficiently. Queues help organize and distribute work items, while Teams group users together based on roles, expertise, or business functions. 1. Defining Queues in D365 Customer Service A Queue is a collection of work items (Cases, Activities, Emails, etc.) that can be assigned to users or teams for resolution. Steps to Create a Queue Go to the Power Platform Admin Center → Select your D365 Customer Service App. Navigate to Customer Service Hub → Service Management → Queues. Click New to create a new queue. Fill in the details:Name: E.g., "Warranty Claims Queue" Type:Public: Any user can work on queue items. Private: Only assigned users can access the queue.   Incoming Email (if using email-to-case routing). Associated Team (Optional): Links the queue to a team for better work distribution.   Save and Close.  Types of Queues Default Queue: Assigned automatically to each user and team. Custom Queues: Specific to business processes (e.g., "High Priority Cases"). Routing Queues: Used in Omnichannel for automated case routing.  2. Defining Teams in D365 Customer Service A Team is a group of users who share ownership of records and work collaboratively. Steps to Create a Team Go to Power Platform Admin Center → Advanced Settings. Navigate to Settings → Security → Teams. Click New and enter:Team Name: E.g., "Warranty Support Team" Business Unit: Assign to the correct unit. Team Type:Owner Team (Default) – Can own records. Access Team – Can only collaborate but not own records.   Queue Association: (Optional) Assign a default queue for the team.   Add Users to the Team. Save and Close.

l3Processes.title: Assign a case

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description: As a Customer Support Manager, I want to assign cases to the appropriate team, individual, or queue so that cases are handled by the right people and resolved efficiently. Process for Assigning a Case to a Team, Individual, or Queue in D365 Customer Service Assigning cases correctly in D365 Customer Service ensures that the right team, individual, or queue handles the case efficiently, improving response times and resolution rates. 1. Steps to Assign a Case to a Team, Individual, or Queue Step 1: Open the Case Record Navigate to Customer Service Workspace. In the left navigation, go to Cases. Open the case that needs to be assigned.  Step 2: Choose Assignment Method A. Assign to an Individual Click on the Assign button in the top action bar. In the Assign To window, select User. Search for and select the User to assign the case to. Click Assign.The case is now owned by the selected individual.  B. Assign to a Team Click Assign. In the Assign To window, select Team. Search for and select the Team. Click Assign.The case is now owned by the selected team, and any team member can work on it.  C. Assign to a Queue (For Automatic or Shared Work Handling) Open the Case Record. Locate the Queue field. Click Change Queue and select the appropriate queue. Click Save & Close.✅ The case is now assigned to a queue, and available agents can pick it up.  Step 3: Automate Assignment Using Routing Rules (Optional) If cases should be assigned automatically, configure Routing Rules: Navigate to Customer Service Admin Center. Go to Work Distribution & Routing → Routing Rule Sets. Define rules based on case priority, type, or customer. Assign cases directly to a queue, team, or user.Cases are now automatically routed based on predefined rules.

l3Processes.title: Define routing rules

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.parentL2: Define customer and employee service operations
l3Processes.description:
Routing rules in Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps determine how cases
are automatically assigned to agents, teams, or queues based on predefined
criteria such as case type, priority, customer segment, or required skills. The
system supports rule-based routing, escalation logic, and exception handling.
Routing rules can be configured to optimize response times, balance workloads,
and ensure cases are handled by the most appropriate resources.

l3Processes.title: Determine business process flow

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.parentL2: Define customer and employee service operations
l3Processes.description: As a Customer Service Manager, I want to implement a Business Process Flow (BPF) for case management so that agents follow a structured, step-by-step approach to resolving customer issues efficiently and consistently  Steps to Define a Business Process Flow (BPF) in D365 Customer Service A Business Process Flow (BPF) in Dynamics 365 Customer Service guides users through a structured sequence of stages to ensure consistent case resolution. 1. Access the Business Process Flow Designer Navigate to Power Apps (https://make.powerapps.com). Select the Environment where you want to create the BPF. Go to Solutions (or create a new solution to store the BPF). Click New → Select Process. In the Create Process dialog:Process Name: e.g., "Case Resolution Process" Category: Business Process Flow Entity: Case Click Create.    2. Define Stages & Steps in the Business Process Flow A BPF consists of Stages (phases) and Steps (fields to be filled in each stage). Example Stages for Case Management: Identify Issue Step 1: Enter Case Title (Required). Step 2: Select Case Type (Problem, Request, Inquiry).   Investigate Step 1: Assign to an Agent or Queue. Step 2: Capture Customer Notes or Attachments. Step 3: Set Case Priority.   Resolve Step 1: Enter Resolution Details. Step 2: Confirm Customer Satisfaction.   Close Case Step 1: Mark Case as Resolved or Escalated. Step 2: Generate Closure Summary Report.    3. Configure Conditional Branching (If Needed) If Case Type = "High Priority", route it to a Supervisor Review stage. If Case Resolution Time > 24 hours, escalate the case automatically.  To configure branching: Click the Stage where you want a decision point. Click Add Condition. Define logic (e.g., "If Case Priority = High, move to Escalation Stage"). Apply conditions and save.  4. Set Required Fields & Permissions Mark important fields as Required to ensure data entry before moving to the next stage. Assign security roles to control who can modify the BPF.  5. Activate and Publish the BPF Click Validate to check for errors. Click Save and then Activate. Publish the BPF so users can see it in Case Records.  Bonus: Tracking & Reporting on BPF Progress Use Dashboards to track how long cases stay in each stage. Create Power BI reports to analyze case resolution efficiency.

l3Processes.title: Define KPIs for call center performance

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.parentL2: Define customer and employee service operations
l3Processes.description:
Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for call center performance are metrics used
to measure the effectiveness and efficiency of case handling. Dynamics 365
finance and operations apps supports the definition and tracking of KPIs such as
case resolution time, first contact resolution rate, customer satisfaction
scores, and agent productivity. KPIs can be visualized in dashboards and used to
drive continuous improvement.

l3Processes.title: Define operating hours

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.parentL2: Define customer and employee service operations
l3Processes.description: As a Customer Service Manager, I want to define operating hours for our support team so that customer cases, SLAs, and agent availability align with our business schedule.  Determining Operating Hours in D365 Customer Service Operating hours in Dynamics 365 Customer Service define the availability of support teams, queues, and service-level agreements (SLAs). They ensure that case escalations, response times, and agent availability are aligned with business hours. 1. Steps to Define Operating Hours in D365 Step 1: Navigate to the Customer Service Admin Center Sign in to Power Platform Admin Center (https://admin.powerplatform.microsoft.com). Select your D365 environment. Open the Customer Service Admin Center or Omnichannel Admin Center.  Step 2: Create Operating Hours Navigate to Work Distribution & Routing → Operating Hours. Click New and enter:Name: (e.g., "Standard Business Hours" or "24/7 Support Hours"). Time Zone: Select the applicable time zone.   Under Weekly Schedule, define working hours for each day:Example:Monday - Friday: 8:00 AM - 6:00 PM Saturday - Sunday: Closed     If 24/7 Support is required, select Always Open. Click Save.  Step 3: Define Holiday Schedules (If Applicable) In the Operating Hours record, navigate to Holidays. Click New Holiday and define:Holiday Name (e.g., "New Year's Day"). Date & Time when support is unavailable.   Save and repeat for other holidays.  Step 4: Assign Operating Hours to Entities Queues: Ensure that case routing respects working hours. SLAs: Define business hours for response time tracking. Omnichannel Workstreams: Control when live chat and phone support are available.  Step 5: Validate and Publish Check that queues and SLAs reference the correct Operating Hours. Test by creating a Case or Work Item outside business hours to verify behavior.

l3Processes.title: Define case types and policies

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.parentL2: Define customer and employee service operations
l3Processes.description: Defining Case Types and Policies in D365 Customer Service Defining Case Types and Policies ensures that customer issues are categorized correctly and handled according to standardized processes. Case Types classify cases (e.g., inquiries, complaints, service requests), while Policies dictate resolution procedures, escalations, and SLA adherence. 1. Steps to Define Case Types in D365 Step 1: Navigate to Power Apps for Case Type Configuration Go to Power Apps (https://make.powerapps.com). Select the Environment where you want to configure case types. Click on Tables in the left navigation. Search for and select the Case (incident) table.  Step 2: Create a Case Type Choice (Option Set Field) In the Case table, go to the Columns tab. Find the Case Type column (or create a new Choice column if not available). Click Edit Choice and define case type values:Inquiry Service Request Complaint Technical Issue   Click Save & Publish.  2. Steps to Define Case Subjects in D365 Step 1: Navigate to Customer Service Admin Center Go to Customer Service Admin Center (https://admin.powerplatform.microsoft.com). Select your Environment and open Customer Support. Navigate to Case Settings → Case Subjects.  Step 2: Define Case Subjects (Hierarchical Categories) Click New Subject and enter:Name: (e.g., "Product Issues", "Billing", "Software Bug"). Parent Subject (Optional for nested categories).   Click Save & Close.  3. Steps to Define Case Policies In Case Settings, configure the following options: Case Handling Times Define response and resolution time expectations. Configure SLAs to track service commitments.   Advanced Similarity Rules Enable AI-based duplicate case detection to suggest related cases. Improve efficiency by preventing duplicate case creation.   Automatic Record Creation & Update Rules Automate case creation from emails, chats, and other channels. Define rules to update existing cases based on new customer interactions.   Surveys & Customer Feedback Set up customer satisfaction surveys for post-resolution feedback. Integrate with Microsoft Forms or Customer Voice.   Other Settings Define default case resolution behaviors. Set up auto-close rules for resolved cases. Configure notifications for escalations and SLA breaches.

l3Processes.title: Capture details about a case

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description: As a Customer Support Agent, I want to capture detailed case information so that cases are well-documented, easy to track, and efficiently resolved.  Steps for Capturing Details About a Case in D365 Customer Service Capturing detailed and accurate information about a Case in D365 Customer Service is essential for effective resolution, tracking, and reporting. 1. Steps to Capture Case Details Step 1: Open the Customer Service Workspace Navigate to Customer Service Workspace. Click on Cases from the left navigation panel. Click New Case to create a new record.  Step 2: Enter Case Information Customer Information Select Customer (Account or Contact). Add Primary Contact (if applicable).   Case Classification Enter Case Title (e.g., "Billing Discrepancy Issue"). Select Case Type (e.g., Inquiry, Complaint, Service Request). Choose a Case Subject from the defined hierarchy. Assign Priority (Low, Normal, High, Critical).   Case Origin Select Case Source (e.g., Phone, Email, Web, Chat, Social Media). If the case was automatically created, review auto-filled details.   Description of the Issue Provide a detailed description of the customer's issue. Attach screenshots, documents, or videos if applicable.   Assign the Case (Optional) Assign to a specific agent, team, or queue for handling. Use routing rules if automatic assignment is enabled.    Step 3: Capture Additional Case Details Related Cases or Similar Issues Use AI-based Similar Case Detection to find related cases. Link relevant knowledge articles if available.   Service-Level Agreements (SLAs) Verify response time requirements based on customer SLAs. Ensure SLA timers are tracking appropriately.   Customer Entitlements (If Applicable) Check entitlements to see the level of support the customer is eligible for.   Case Timeline & Notes Log agent interactions, customer follow-ups, and status updates. Record any internal comments for reference.    Step 4: Save & Track Case Progress Click Save & Close or Save & Continue. The case is now available for agents to update, resolve, or escalate. Use Dashboards & Reports to track case resolution times and trends.

l3Processes.title: Create a case from a channel

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description: As a Customer Support Manager, I want to ensure that cases are automatically created from various customer channels so that issues are tracked efficiently and resolved promptly.   Steps to Create a Case from Different Channels in D365 Customer Service Cases can be created manually by agents or automatically when customer interactions come from various channels like chat, phone calls, portal requests, emails, and product returns. Steps to Create a Case from Different Channels  A. Create a Case from a Chat (Omnichannel for Customer Service) Enable Omnichannel for Customer Service in Customer Service Admin Center. Customer starts a chat session via Live Chat, SMS, or WhatsApp. Agent receives the chat in the Omnichannel Agent Dashboard. Click Create Case directly from the chat window. The system auto-fills:Customer Name (if authenticated). Case Origin = Chat. Conversation Details from the chat.   Agent enters additional details and clicks Save & Close.The case is now linked to the chat conversation.  B. Create a Case from a Live Phone Call in Omnichannel Open Omnichannel for Customer Service. Navigate to the Omnichannel Agent Dashboard. When a phone call request arrives, click Accept. The call session will open in the conversation panel. Click More Actions (⋮) → Create Case. A new Case Form will open with the following auto-filled details:Customer Name Case Origin = Phone Call Call Transcript or Notes (if enabled)   Enter additional details:Case Title (brief issue summary). Case Type (Inquiry, Complaint, Service Request, etc.). Priority (Low, Normal, High, Critical). Detailed Description of the issue.   Assign the case to a specific agent, team, or queue (or let Routing Rules assign it automatically). Click Save & Close to link the case to the phone call session.The phone call is logged, and the case is linked for further resolution.  C. Create a Case from a Portal Request Customer submits a request via the Customer Self-Service Portal. The system automatically creates a case when the request is submitted. The Case Record is auto-populated with:Customer Name & Contact Info. Case Title & Description (from the form submission). Case Origin = Portal.   Case routing rules assign the case to a team or queue. Customer receives an automated email confirmation.The case is now in the system and ready for an agent to process.  D. Create a Case from an Email Enable Email-to-Case Automation in Customer Service Admin Center. Customer sends an email to a designated support email address. The system automatically:Creates a Case Record. Links the email conversation to the case. Sets Case Origin = Email.   The case is placed in the Email Queue, ready for assignment.Agents can now respond directly from the case record.

l3Processes.title: Create a case manually

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description: As a Customer Support Agent, I want to manually create cases for different customer concerns so that issues are properly categorized, assigned, and resolved.   Steps to Manually Create a Case in D365 Customer Service Creating a case manually ensures that customer issues are properly tracked and assigned, especially for cases such as customer complaints, product returns, and disputed invoices. 1. Steps to Manually Create a Case Step 1: Open Customer Service Workspace Navigate to Customer Service Workspace in Dynamics 365. Click on Cases from the left navigation panel. Click New Case to open the case creation form.  Step 2: Enter General Case Information Customer Information Select Customer Name (Contact or Account). Add Primary Contact (if applicable).   Case Classification Case Title → Briefly describe the issue (e.g., "Damaged Product Received"). Case Type → Select from predefined categories (e.g., Complaint, Product Return, Billing Issue). Case Subject → Select a relevant subject (e.g., "Shipping Issues", "Billing Disputes"). Priority → Set based on urgency (Low, Normal, High, Critical).   Case Origin Choose how the issue was reported (Phone, Email, Chat, Web, In-Person).    Step 3: Capture Specific Case Details Based on Type A. Example based on customer complaint Case Type: Complaint Description: Document the customer’s concern in detail. Attachments: Upload any supporting files (screenshots, email evidence). Resolution Expectations: Document what the customer expects as a resolution. Assign Case:Assign to Customer Relations Team or a specific agent. Use Routing Rules if automated assignment is enabled.   Click Save & Close or Save & Continue.The customer complaint case is logged and ready for resolution.  B. Example based on Product Return Case Case Type: Product Return Order Number: Link the case to the corresponding order. Reason for Return: Select from predefined options (Defective, Wrong Item, Not Needed). Return Process:Attach return documents (receipts, images of the product). Provide shipping instructions if necessary.   Assign Case:Assign to Returns Processing Team. Move the case to a Product Return Queue.   Click Save & Close.The product return case is created and linked to the order.  C. Example based on Disputed Invoice Case Case Type: Billing Issue Invoice Number: Enter or search for the disputed invoice. Dispute Reason: Select from predefined options (Incorrect Amount, Duplicate Charge, Unauthorized Charge). Supporting Evidence: Attach relevant documents (invoice copy, payment receipt, customer email). Resolution Process:Specify the next steps (review invoice, credit/refund processing). Assign case to Billing/Accounts Team.   Click Save & Close.The disputed invoice case is logged and ready for financial review.  Step 4: Follow Up & Track Case Progress Use Case Timeline to document updates and customer follow-ups. Attach relevant Knowledge Base Articles for agent reference. Set up SLA tracking to ensure timely resolution. If needed, escalate the case to a higher-priority team.

l3Processes.title: Send notification to customer

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description: As a Customer Service Representative (CSR),I want to automatically notify customers when a new case is created, updated, or resolved,So that I can ensure timely communication, reduce manual effort, and improve customer satisfaction. Process for Sending Customer Notifications in D365 CE for Case Management This document outlines the process and detailed steps for automatically sending notifications to customers in Microsoft Dynamics 365 Customer Engagement (D365 CE) when a new case is created, updated, or resolved. 1. Process Overview Notification Scenarios New Case Acknowledgment: When a new case is created, the customer should receive a confirmation email with case details. Case Update Notification: When a case is updated (status change, assigned agent change, or important notes added), the customer should be informed. Case Resolution Notification: When a case is resolved or closed, the customer should receive a resolution summary.  Technology Used Power Automate: Automates email notifications triggered by case events. D365 Email Templates: Predefined templates ensure consistent messaging. Dataverse Triggers: Listens for case events in the system. SMS Gateway (Optional): For SMS notifications if required.  2. Process Workflow Step 1: Trigger Setup (Define Event) Define the trigger event for each type of notification: New Case Acknowledgment → Triggered when a new case is created. Case Update Notification → Triggered when a case status, owner, or priority is updated. Case Resolution Notification → Triggered when a case is marked as "Resolved" or "Closed."  Step 2: Fetch Customer Details Retrieve the customer's email or preferred notification channel from the Case record. Get related Account or Contact information.  Step 3: Select Notification Template Use Email Templates in D365 CE for consistent messaging. Personalize the template with:Case Number Case Title Current Status Assigned Agent Resolution Summary (for closed cases) Case Portal Link (if applicable)    Step 4: Send Notification via Power Automate New Case Acknowledgment: Trigger: When a new case is created. Action: Send an email to the customer with the case number and SLA details.   Case Update Notification: Trigger: When an important field changes (status, priority, owner, or a significant note is added). Action: Send an update email or SMS with the latest case details.   Case Resolution Notification: Trigger: When a case status is changed to "Resolved" or "Closed." Action: Send a resolution summary to the customer with next steps or feedback request.    Step 5: Log Notification in Case Timeline Create a Case Timeline entry in D365 CE for audit purposes. Store notification details, including:Timestamp Email content Customer response (if applicable)    3. Detailed Steps in Power Automate Below are the detailed steps for implementing this using Power Automate. A. Flow for New Case Acknowledgment Trigger: When a new Case is created in D365 CE (Dataverse trigger). Action: Retrieve related Contact/Account details. Action: Retrieve the "New Case Acknowledgment" email template. Action: Personalize the email with case details. Action: Send an email to the customer. Action (Optional): Send an SMS if preferred. Action: Log the notification in the Case Timeline.  B. Flow for Case Update Notification Trigger: When a Case record is updated (Dataverse modified trigger). Condition: Check if key fields changed (Status, Priority, Assigned Agent, Notes). Action: Retrieve the "Case Update" email template. Action: Send the email to the customer. Action: Log the notification in the Case Timeline.  C. Flow for Case Resolution Notification Trigger: When a Case status changes to "Resolved" or "Closed." Action: Retrieve the "Case Resolution" email template. Action: Personalize the email with resolution details and next steps. Action: Send an email with a customer feedback link. Action: Log the notification in the Case Timeline.

l3Processes.title: Engage in conversations with customers

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description: As a Customer Service Representative (CSR), I want to communicate with customers via chat, email, phone, or in-person, So that I can provide timely support and ensure cases are handled efficiently.  his document outlines the standard process and steps for handling customer cases in Microsoft Dynamics 365 Customer Service, including engaging with customers via chat, email, phone, or in-person interactions. 1. Process Overview The case management process ensures efficient tracking, resolution, and communication with customers across multiple channels. The key steps include: Case Creation – Capture customer issues from different channels. Case Triage & Assignment – Prioritize and assign the case to the right agent. Customer Engagement & Communication – Interact with the customer through their preferred channel (chat, email, phone, or in-person). Case Investigation & Resolution – Diagnose and work towards resolving the issue. Case Closure & Follow-up – Provide resolution confirmation and gather customer feedback.  2. Detailed Steps in the Case Management Process Step 1: Case Creation Cases can be created manually by a CSR, automatically from customer interactions, or via a self-service portal. Supported case creation methods:Email: An email received creates a case (via Email-to-Case automation). Chat: Live chat sessions initiate a case. Phone Call: A CSR logs a case from an inbound or outbound call. In-Person: A CSR manually logs the issue after an in-person conversation. Self-Service Portal: Customers submit cases via a support portal.    Action Items: Ensure customer details (contact, account, and issue description) are correctly captured. Assign a priority and category for the case.   Step 2: Case Triage & Assignment Automatic Assignment: Based on skill set, availability, or workload via Omnichannel for Customer Service or Workstreams. Manual Assignment: A supervisor or dispatcher assigns the case. Prioritization Rules: Cases are categorized as Low, Normal, High, or Urgent based on impact.  Action Items:Ensure the correct agent is assigned. Add internal notes if required for additional context.   Step 3: Customer Engagement & Communication The agent initiates or continues communication with the customer based on their preferred channel: 1. Live Chat (Omnichannel for Customer Service) Customer starts a conversation via chat. The CSR greets the customer and reviews previous interactions (if available). CSR works on resolving the issue in real-time. If further action is needed, the chat is converted into a case for follow-up.  2. Email (Customer Timeline in D365) CSR responds to customer emails directly within the Case record. Email templates can be used for standard responses. Attachments (screenshots, guides, etc.) can be included. If necessary, an escalation email is sent to internal teams.  3. Phone Call (Integrated Softphone or Manual Entry) CSR calls the customer and logs the conversation in the Case Timeline. Key details such as call duration, discussion points, and next steps are recorded. If needed, the call is transferred to a different team or escalated.  4. In-Person Meeting (Field Visit or Walk-in) If the customer visits the support center or an agent is dispatched onsite, notes are logged. Customer signs off on discussions or agreements, if applicable. Any necessary follow-up actions are scheduled.  Action Items:Keep conversation history logged in the Case Timeline for visibility. Ensure follow-up tasks (if needed) are scheduled.   Step 4: Case Investigation & Resolution The CSR diagnoses the issue using knowledge articles, past cases, or internal resources. If needed, the case is escalated to technical support or a manager. If a product return, refund, or service visit is required, the proper process is initiated. A resolution is determined and communicated to the customer.  Action Items:Use Knowledge Base articles to assist in troubleshooting. Escalate the case if additional expertise is required. Document resolution steps in the case record.   Step 5: Case Closure & Follow-up The agent confirms with the customer that the issue is resolved. The case is marked as “Resolved” in D365 Customer Service. A feedback survey is sent to the customer (optional). If unresolved, the case is reopened for further action.  Action Items:Update the case with final resolution notes. Notify the customer and confirm resolution before closing the case. (Optional) Send a post-resolution survey for customer feedback.

l3Processes.title: Create and track tasks for a case

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description: As a Customer Service Representative (CSR),I want to create and track tasks related to a case,So that I can ensure all necessary actions are completed for resolution.  Steps to Create and Track Tasks for a Case in D365 Customer Service In Microsoft Dynamics 365 Customer Service, tasks help agents track actions related to resolving customer cases. This document outlines the steps to create, assign, and track tasks associated with a case. 1. Process Overview When working on a case, tasks can be created to: Assign follow-up actions to an agent or team. Track pending actions such as investigations, customer callbacks, or approvals. Ensure that all case-related activities are completed before closing the case.  Tasks can be managed through the Activities pane in the Case record. 2. Steps to Create a Task for a Case Step 1: Navigate to the Case Record Open Dynamics 365 Customer Service. Go to Service > Cases. Select an existing case or create a new one.  Step 2: Add a New Task Within the Case record, locate the Timeline or Activities section.  Click + Add Activity and select Task.  Enter task details: Subject: A brief task name (e.g., "Follow up with customer"). Description: Additional details on what needs to be done. Owner: Assign the task to a specific agent or team. Due Date: Set a deadline for task completion. Priority: Choose Low, Normal, or High. Regarding: Ensure the case record is linked to the task.   Click Save & Close to create the task.   3. Steps to Track and Update Tasks Step 1: View Tasks Related to a Case Open the Case record in D365. In the Timeline section, locate all open tasks. Use filters to view Active Tasks only.  Step 2: Update Task Progress Click on an existing task to open it.  Modify details such as status, notes, or due date.  Change the Status Reason: Not Started (default) In Progress Waiting for Input (if waiting for a customer or internal team) Completed (when done)   Click Save & Close to update the task.   4. Steps to Complete a Task and Close It Open the task from the Case Timeline. Change Status to Completed. Add final notes in the Description field if needed. Click Save & Close.  5. Automating Task Creation (Optional) Use Power Automate to automatically create tasks when: A case is assigned to a specific queue. A case reaches a certain status (e.g., "Waiting for Customer Response"). A high-priority case is created.

l3Processes.title: Follow up on a case

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description: As a Customer Service Representative (CSR),I want to follow up on a case internally, with a customer, or with a vendor,So that I can ensure timely updates, track progress, and resolve the issue efficiently.  Steps to Follow Up on a Case in D365 Customer Service Effective case follow-ups ensure timely resolution and maintain strong communication between internal teams, customers, and vendors. This guide outlines the steps for: Internal Case Follow-up (within the organization) Customer Case Follow-up (keeping customers informed) Vendor Case Follow-up (escalating cases to third-party vendors)  1. Internal Case Follow-up (Within the Organization) Step 1: Identify Follow-up Needs Open the Case Record in D365 Customer Service. Check the Status, Assigned Agent, and Notes to determine if internal follow-up is required.  Step 2: Assign or Escalate the Case If additional expertise is needed, assign the case to another team or escalate. Use the Case Owner field to change ownership. Add an Internal Note with instructions or context.  Step 3: Schedule a Task for Internal Follow-up Click + Add Activity > Task within the Case Timeline. Assign the task to an internal team member with a due date. Mark the task as In Progress or Waiting for Input.  Step 4: Follow-up via Teams or Email Send an internal email by clicking Email in the Activities section. If immediate attention is required, initiate a Microsoft Teams chat with the assigned agent or team.  Step 5: Monitor Status & Close Internal Task Review the case status and activity updates. Once internal feedback is received, close the task and proceed with external follow-up.  2. Customer Case Follow-up Step 1: Check Case Progress Open the Case Record and review the Case Timeline. Identify pending actions or updates.  Step 2: Choose Follow-up Method Email: Use email templates for consistency. Phone Call: Log the details in Case Activities. Live Chat: Update the customer in real-time (if using Omnichannel).  Step 3: Send an Update Click + Add Activity > Email within the Case. Select an Email Template or write a custom response. Include:Case Number Current Status Next Steps & Expected Resolution Time Customer Portal Link (if applicable)   Click Send & Track Response.  Step 4: Log Customer Response If the customer responds, update the case notes in the Timeline. If additional action is required, create a Task or reassign the case.  Step 5: Set a Reminder for Next Follow-up If no immediate resolution is available, create a follow-up task. Mark the next follow-up date and notify the assigned agent.  3. Vendor Case Follow-up (Suitable for cases involving third-party vendors, suppliers, or service providers.) Step 1: Gather Vendor Contact Information Open the Case Record and check for linked vendor details. If the vendor is not listed, locate the contact under Accounts or Contacts.  Step 2: Send a Vendor Inquiry Click + Add Activity > Email and address it to the vendor contact. Include:Case Reference Number Issue Summary Required Action from the Vendor Deadline for Response    Step 3: Follow-up with a Phone Call (if needed) If no response is received within the SLA, call the vendor. Log the phone call under Activities > Phone Call.  Step 4: Update the Case Record Document the vendor’s response and next steps in the Case Timeline. If additional vendor actions are required, set a Follow-up Task.  Step 5: Notify the Customer (if applicable) If the vendor provides a resolution, update the customer. If delays occur, inform the customer with an expected timeline.  As a Customer Service Representative (CSR),I want to follow up on a case internally, with a customer, or with a vendor,So that I can ensure timely updates, track progress, and resolve the issue efficiently.

l3Processes.title: Merge cases

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description: As a Customer Service Representative (CSR), I want to merge duplicate cases into a single case,So that I can avoid redundancy and improve case tracking. Steps to Merge Cases in D365 Customer Service In Microsoft Dynamics 365 Customer Service, merging duplicate or related cases ensures a streamlined case management process by consolidating information into a single case record. 1. When to Merge Cases Merging is useful when:A customer submits multiple cases for the same issue.Two agents create separate cases for the same customer request.A case is mistakenly duplicated. 2. Steps to Merge Cases in D365 Customer Service Step 1: Open the Case List View Navigate to Service > Cases in D365. Identify the cases you want to merge.  Step 2: Select Cases to Merge Select the primary case (the case that will remain). Select the secondary case(s) (the ones to be merged into the primary case). Click on the "Merge Cases" option in the command bar.  Step 3: Confirm the Merge Details A confirmation dialog will appear. Choose how to handle activities:Keep activities and merge them into the primary case. Close activities in the secondary case(s).   Confirm that the selected primary case is correct. Click Merge.  Step 4: Verify the Merged Case Open the primary case. Ensure that:Notes, emails, and activities from the secondary case(s) have been transferred. The case status and owner remain unchanged.   The merged cases are now marked as Canceled and linked in the Case Timeline.  3. Important Considerations  Merging is irreversible – verify case details before merging. All activities from secondary cases will be linked to the primary case. SLAs on the primary case remain active; merged cases lose their SLA tracking. Security roles must allow merging cases.

l3Processes.title: Re-assign case to another team/individual

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description: As a Customer Service Supervisor, I want to reassign cases to the correct team or agent,So that the issue is handled efficiently by the appropriate resource. Steps to Reassign Cases to Another Team or Individual in D365 Customer Service Reassigning cases in Microsoft Dynamics 365 Customer Service ensures that the right team or agent handles the issue efficiently. This guide outlines the steps to reassign a case to another team or individual. 1. When to Reassign a Case The case was assigned to the wrong agent.A different team specializes in the issue.The original agent is unavailable.The case requires escalation to a senior agent or supervisor. 2. Steps to Reassign a Case in D365 Customer Service Step 1: Open the Case Record Navigate to Service > Cases in D365. Locate the case that needs reassignment. Click to open the case details.  Step 2: Click on "Assign" In the command bar, click Assign. A new window will appear with assignment options.  Step 3: Choose the New Owner To assign the case to an individual:Select User, then choose the appropriate agent from the list.   To assign the case to a team:Select Team, then pick the relevant support team.    Step 4: Click "Assign" The case ownership will be transferred immediately. The new owner or team will receive a notification (if configured).  3. Tracking and Managing Reassigned Cases View Assigned Cases: In the Case list, filter by "Owner" to see cases assigned to specific users/teams.Monitor SLAs: Ensure reassigned cases still meet SLA timelines.Update Case Notes: Document why the case was reassigned for transparency.

l3Processes.title: Send knowledge article to customer

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description: As a Customer Service Representative (CSR), I want to send a relevant Knowledge Article to a customer,So that they can quickly find answers and resolve their issue.  Steps to Send a Knowledge Article to a Customer in D365 Customer Service Sharing a Knowledge Article with a customer helps provide quick resolutions and improve case efficiency. This guide outlines the steps to find, attach, and send a Knowledge Article in Microsoft Dynamics 365 Customer Service. 1. When to Send a Knowledge Article  The article contains troubleshooting steps relevant to the case. The customer has a frequently asked question. The issue can be resolved using self-help instructions. The agent wants to provide additional reference material.   2. Steps to Send a Knowledge Article in D365 Customer Service Step 1: Open the Case Record Navigate to Service > Cases in D365. Select the case where you want to send an article. Open the case details page.  Step 2: Search for a Knowledge Article In the Case Form, locate the Knowledge Search pane (usually on the right side). Type relevant keywords related to the issue. Select the most appropriate Knowledge Article. Click Associate to link the article to the case.  Step 3: Send the Knowledge Article to the Customer Option 1: Send via Email Click Email a link or Attach to Email (based on your setup). A new email window will open with the article attached or linked. Personalize the email with:Case Number Customer Greeting Brief Explanation of the Article   Click Send.  Option 2: Send via Chat (Omnichannel for Customer Service) If the customer is engaged in a live chat, select the article. Click Send as a Chat Message. The article link will be sent instantly in the chat.  Option 3: Copy & Share the Public URL Open the Knowledge Article. Copy the public URL (if available). Paste it into an email, chat, or case notes for the customer.  3. Tracking and Managing Sent Articles Check Email Activity – Ensure the article was sent successfully. Monitor Customer Engagement – If the customer hasn’t responded, follow up. Log Article Usage – Track how often a Knowledge Article is used.

l3Processes.title: Swarm on case with team

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description: As a Customer Service Agent, I want to collaborate with my team using a case swarm,So that I can resolve complex issues faster with expert input.  Steps to SWARM on a Case with a Team in D365 Customer Service A Case Swarm is a collaborative approach where multiple agents, teams, or experts work together on a complex case to expedite resolution. In Microsoft Dynamics 365 Customer Service, SWARMING ensures that difficult cases get the attention they need from the right people. 1. When to SWARM on a Case The case is complex and requires input from multiple experts.A high-priority or VIP customer case needs immediate resolution.The assigned agent lacks the expertise to resolve the issue alone.The issue spans multiple departments (e.g., IT, Billing, Customer Support). 2. Steps to Initiate a Case SWARM in D365 Customer Service Step 1: Identify the Need for a Swarm Open the Case Record and review the issue details. Check if previous troubleshooting attempts were unsuccessful. Determine the required expertise (technical support, billing, etc.).  Step 2: Start a Swarm Discussion in Microsoft Teams In the Case Record, click the "Collaborate" (Microsoft Teams Integration) button. Select Start a Teams Chat or Meeting with relevant agents. Tag subject matter experts and provide a brief case summary. Attach relevant case details, notes, or customer history.  Step 3: Assign Tasks to Team Members Within D365 Activities, create Tasks for each contributor. Assign owners, set due dates, and track progress. Use Power Automate to notify team members automatically.  Step 4: Update the Case with Findings & Next Steps As each participant provides input, log updates in the Case Timeline. Summarize key takeaways from the swarm session. Assign follow-ups or escalation steps if needed.  Step 5: Communicate with the Customer Once a resolution path is determined, update the customer. If additional time is needed, provide an estimated resolution timeframe. Send a Knowledge Article if applicable.  Step 6: Close the Swarm & Monitor Case Progress Mark internal tasks as Completed once team contributions are finalized. If resolved, close the case and request customer feedback. If unresolved, escalate the case to higher support levels.

l3Processes.title: Track additional information against a case

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description: As a Customer Service Representative (CSR), I want to attach documents, add notes, and create relationships between cases and other records,So that I can maintain a complete and organized case history for better resolution tracking.  Steps to Track Additional Information Against a Case in D365 Customer Service To ensure comprehensive case management, agents often need to attach documents, add notes, update the timeline, and create relationships between cases or other records in Microsoft Dynamics 365 Customer Service (D365 CE). This guide outlines the steps to track and manage additional case-related information efficiently. 1. Accessing the Case in Customer Service Workspace Step 1: Open the Case in Customer Service Workspace Navigate to Customer Service Workspace in D365. Click on Cases from the left-hand navigation panel. Use the search bar or filters to find the case you need. Click on the case to open the record.  2. Attaching Documents to a Case Step 1: Upload a File to the Case Timeline In the Timeline section, click + Add Note. Type a brief description of the document. Click Attach File, select the file from your system, and click Upload. Click Save to link the document to the case.  Step 2: Upload to SharePoint (if enabled) Scroll to the Documents section in the case form. Click Upload and select the file. The document is now stored in the linked SharePoint folder.  3. Updating the Timeline on a Case Step 1: Add a Note to the Timeline In the Case Record, navigate to the Timeline section. Click + Add Note. Enter relevant case details, updates, or progress tracking. (Optional) Use the "@" symbol to mention a colleague for collaboration. Click Save – the note will now be visible in the Case Timeline.  Step 2: Log an Activity (Email, Phone Call, Task, etc.) In the Timeline, click + Add Activity. Choose the appropriate activity type (e.g., Phone Call, Email, Task). Enter the necessary details:For Phone Calls: Log the conversation details. For Emails: Compose and send an email directly from the case. For Tasks: Assign follow-ups to agents.   Click Save – the activity is now recorded in the Case Timeline.  4. Creating Relationships to Other Records or Cases Step 1: Link a Case to Another Case (Parent/Child Relationship) Open the Case Record that will be the "Child" case. Scroll to the Related Cases section. Click + New Case Relationship. Select the Parent Case from existing records. Click Save & Close.  Use Case: When a case is related to a larger issue, linking it as a Child Case allows tracking under one Parent Case. Step 2: Link a Case to Other Records (Contacts, Accounts, Work Orders, etc.) Open the Case Record. Navigate to the Related Records section. Select the type of record you want to link (e.g., Contacts, Accounts, Work Orders, Opportunities). Click + Add Existing Record or + New Record if it doesn’t exist. Click Save to establish the relationship.  Use Case: If a case is related to a specific customer account, asset, or work order, linking ensures better traceability. 5. Best Practices  Use SharePoint Integration for secure and centralized document storage. Keep Notes Organized by adding timestamps and tagging relevant agents. Log All Interactions to maintain a full history of customer communications. Automate Case Documentation – Use Power Automate to generate reports when a case is updated.

l3Processes.title: Use similar cases to find a solution

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description:
Agents can search for and reference similar cases to inform resolution
strategies. Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps supports case similarity
search and solution reuse.

l3Processes.title: Analyze case patterns

apqc.description: Manage customer service problems, requests, and inquiries

l3Processes.description: As a Customer Service Manager, I want to analyze case patterns and trends,So that I can identify recurring issues, optimize case resolution, and improve customer satisfaction. Steps to Analyze Case Patterns in D365 Customer Service Analyzing case patterns in Microsoft Dynamics 365 Customer Service helps identify recurring issues, optimize resource allocation, and improve service efficiency. This guide outlines how to use Customer Service Historical Analytics to uncover case trends and patterns. 1. Accessing Case Analytics in D365 Step 1: Open Customer Service Historical Analytics Navigate to Customer Service Workspace or Customer Service Hub. Click Customer Service Historical Analytics from the left-hand navigation panel. Open the Case Insights Dashboard.  2. Identifying Case Trends & Recurring Issues Step 2: Review Case Volume Trends Metrics to Analyze: Total Cases Created Over Time – Identify seasonal or recurring spikes. Case Volume by Type – Determine which types of issues occur most often. Case Volume by Channel – Compare issues reported via email, chat, phone, or self-service portal. Top Customers by Case Count – Identify customers submitting frequent cases.  Best Practice: Apply date range filters (weekly, monthly, quarterly) to spot long-term trends. Step 3: Analyze Case Resolution & Escalations Key Metrics: Average Resolution Time – Identify case types taking longer to resolve. Escalation Rate by Case Type – Find case categories that frequently require higher-level intervention. Case Reopen Rate – Detect cases that were closed but needed follow-up.  Best Practice: If certain case types have high escalation or reopen rates, they may require process improvements or updated Knowledge Articles. 3. Analyzing Agent & Team Case Handling Patterns Step 4: Evaluate Agent & Team Performance In Customer Service Historical Analytics, open the Agent Insights dashboard. Filter by Agent Name or Team. Analyze the following KPIs:Average Case Handling Time Per Agent – Identify efficiency gaps. Number of Cases Assigned vs. Resolved – Compare workload vs. output. Escalation Rate Per Agent – Find agents frequently escalating cases.    Best Practice: Compare case resolution trends across different teams or shifts to optimize staffing. 4. Detecting SLA Violations & Resolution Delays Step 5: Identify SLA Compliance Trends Key Metrics: Total SLA Breaches – Cases exceeding response or resolution deadlines. Cases Nearing SLA Deadline – Identify cases at risk of violating SLAs. Case Resolution Time vs. SLA Goal – Compare actual vs. expected resolution performance.  Best Practice: Use Power Automate to notify managers when cases are approaching SLA limits. 5. Leveraging Customer Feedback for Pattern Analysis Step 6: Review Customer Satisfaction (CSAT) Scores Navigate to the CSAT (Customer Satisfaction) Dashboard. Analyze feedback on resolved cases. Identify recurring themes in low-rated cases (e.g., long wait times, incomplete resolutions).  Best Practice: If certain case types consistently get low ratings, investigate process bottlenecks or training needs. 6. Best Practices for Case Pattern Analysis Monitor trends weekly/monthly to detect emerging issues early.Use AI-powered insights (Copilot) to recommend case resolutions.Optimize Knowledge Articles based on case trends.Automate alerts for SLA risks and escalation trends with Power Automate.
