and analyze production processes, the organization can make better decisions
based on real data.
This process area should be implemented whenever any other production processes
are implemented. For implementations that include Dynamics 365 Finance and
Supply Chain Management, production and other supply chain processes are often
part of a second phase. Alternatively, all processes are implemented at once.

l3Processes.title: Correct production processes
l3Processes.description:
This process addresses the need to adjust or improve production workflows based
on feedback, errors, or changing requirements.

l3Processes.title: Modify production plan

apqc.description: Schedule production orders and create lots

l3Processes.description:
Planning production operations picks up where the plan supply and replenishment
process area finishes. Production orders are either firmed from the generated
planned orders or they're manually created. Then, the orders are organized into
a production schedule that often includes sequencing or otherwise organizing
production based on efficiency, due dates, and material and machinery
availability. Once the production schedule is complete for the next time period,
the information is sent to the shop floor, and the run production operations
process area can begin.
This business process area should be included as part of the initial
implementation, unless the business is only implementing financial business
processes in the first phase. Additionally, some organizations choose to
continue with a manual production planning and scheduling process and implement
the functionality in Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management in a later phase. The
production planning functionality is flexible and can be adapted as the business
changes over time.

l3Processes.title: Create production plan

apqc.description: Schedule production orders and create lots

l3Processes.description:
Foundational process of translating demand—whether from customer orders,
forecasts, or internal requirements—into actionable production orders that drive
manufacturing operations. The goal is to ensure that the right products are
produced at the right time, in the right quantities, and with optimal use of
resources, while aligning with business objectives such as cost control,
inventory management, and timely delivery. This process involves identifying
production requirements, developing a plan that sequences and schedules orders,
documenting the plan for visibility and approval, and coordinating with
procurement and inventory to ensure material availability. Scenarios addressed
by "Create Production Plan" include routine manufacturing of standard products,
planning for special or custom orders, accommodating long-lead-time items, and
responding to capital projects or internal requests. The process also supports
the integration of resource constraints, lead times, and prioritization rules,
enabling planners to balance efficiency with responsiveness.

l3Processes.title: Schedule production jobs

apqc.description: Schedule production orders and create lots

l3Processes.description:
This process involves assigning planned production orders or jobs to specific
time slots, work centers, and resources based on demand, resource availability,
and business priorities. This process enables businesses to sequence and
allocate manufacturing tasks efficiently, ensuring that production runs are
coordinated with material availability, labor, and equipment constraints. It
supports the alignment of production activities with customer delivery
requirements, inventory targets, and cost objectives, while providing visibility
into shop floor workloads and potential bottlenecks. By formalizing the
scheduling of production jobs, organizations can improve on-time delivery,
optimize resource utilization, and respond more effectively to changes in demand
or operational disruptions, ultimately supporting agile and reliable
manufacturing operations.

l3Processes.title: Release production to the shop floor

apqc.description: Schedule production orders and create lots

l3Processes.description:
This process in Dynamics 365 Finance & Operations production module involves
transitioning a planned or scheduled production order from the planning phase
into active execution on the shop floor. This process starts when a production
order is confirmed and all necessary materials, drawings, and documentation are
prepared and provided to the production team. The release triggers procurement
of any required raw materials not already in stock, and ensures that all
relevant information—such as shop drawings, specifications, and instructions—is
communicated to the appropriate supervisors or workers. In practice, this step
formalizes the start of manufacturing activities, allowing workers to begin
assembling, fabricating, or processing items according to the order’s
requirements. The process also establishes accountability for tracking progress,
managing inventory consumption, and updating order status as work proceeds. By
releasing production to the shop floor, organizations ensure that production
activities are coordinated, resources are allocated efficiently, and the
transition from planning to execution is clearly documented, supporting accurate
reporting, cost tracking, and timely fulfillment of customer or internal
orders.

l3Processes.title: Estimate the cost of production

apqc.description: Schedule production orders and create lots

l3Processes.description:
The process of "Estimate the cost of production" within the planning production
operations area involves calculating the anticipated expenses required to
manufacture a product or complete a specific job. This estimation typically
includes the costs of raw materials, labor, overhead, and any subcontracted
services, and is based on the bill of materials, historical data, and current
pricing. For standard production, organizations rely on established cost
structures and known material requirements, while for custom or special jobs,
the estimation process may require gathering quotes from vendors, assessing
labor hours, and factoring in unique project requirements. The estimated cost is
documented, reviewed, and often submitted for approval—especially when quoting
to customers or planning for internal budgeting.

l3Processes.title: Start production
l3Processes.description:
The run production operations business process area covers the activities in an
organization's internal production process. Any manufacturing organization finds
it critical to track and control production operations. The goal is to ensure
consistent product quality and minimize production costs. Running production
operations also requires coordination and communication between many
departments, such as engineering, operations, quality, sales, planning, and
procurement.
The process for executing production operations varies from business to
business, depending on industry and product, but it follows the same basic
steps. At the beginning of production, materials are consumed into the process.
A combination of people and tools or machines then run the specific production
steps. Finally, the sub-assembly or finished good is created as the output of
the process. The definition of these steps is done as part of the Design to
retire end-to-end business processes and refined further in the define
production strategies business process area.
Define this business process at the beginning of an implementation of Dynamics
365 Supply Chain Management. It's a foundational process for any manufacturing
company.

l3Processes.title: Adjust production plan
l3Processes.description:
Adjusting the production plan in Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps refers
to modifying scheduled production orders or priorities in response to changing
requirements, emergencies, or material availability. In the meeting, adjustments
are made manually by supervisors based on order priority or urgent needs, rather
than system-driven planning.

l3Processes.title: Consume materials
l3Processes.description:
Consuming materials in Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps means recording
the use of raw materials against production orders as work progresses.

l3Processes.title: Consume resources
l3Processes.description:
Consuming resources refers to logging labor hours and machine usage against
production orders.

l3Processes.title: Report production output
l3Processes.description:
Reporting production output in Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps involves
marking production orders as complete, recording finished goods, and updating
inventory.

l3Processes.title: Subcontract production
l3Processes.description:
Subcontracting production in Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps covers
sending parts of production (e.g., heat treating, powder coating) to external
vendors, managing requisitions, RFQs, and purchase orders.

l3Processes.title: Record production costs
l3Processes.description:
Recording production costs in Dynamics 365 finance and operations apps means
capturing material, labor, and overhead costs for each production order,
supporting cost analysis and financial reporting.

l3Processes.title: Quarantine manufactured goods
l3Processes.description:
This process area is highly related to the Manage inventory quality process area
in the Inventory to deliver end-to-end process. Controlling production quality
covers the processes from inspecting manufactured goods to implementing
corrective and preventive actions to improve product quality.
For regulated industries, this business process area should be considered
critical. It should be addressed initially and continuously referenced
throughout the implementation to ensure compliance. In other organizations, this
process area may be implemented alongside the Run production operations process
area. Also, some companies run production quality processes externally to their
systems and onboard to Dynamics 365 at a later date. If an organization has
requirements to implement the control production quality process area, it's
important to identify that as early as possible because it can increase
implementation scope. It's also important to recognize that these processes are
often executed in "best of breed" specialized systems that are unique by
industry. In that case, integrations to such systems may be required as part of
the Dynamics 365 implementation.

l3Processes.title: Analyze production costs
l3Processes.description:
Systematically reviewing and evaluating all expenses associated with
manufacturing or fabricating products, including raw materials, labor, overhead,
and any variable costs. This process typically includes comparing actual costs
incurred during production to standard or estimated costs, identifying
variances, and understanding the reasons behind cost fluctuations. The analysis
may use production reports, financial statements, and spreadsheets to track cost
trends over time, assess profitability, and inform pricing strategies. It also
helps organizations ensure that their cost structures remain competitive and
sustainable, and supports decision-making for future production planning and
resource allocation.

l3Processes.title: Issue requests for information
l3Processes.parentL2: Source and contract goods and services
l3Processes.description: Initiates supplier engagement by gathering preliminary information about capabilities, products, services, and qualifications. RFIs are used to explore market options, assess supplier fit, and inform future sourcing strategies. Scope & Outcomes  Collects non-binding supplier data  Assesses supplier capabilities, experience, and interest  Informs RFQ/RFP strategy  Outcomes include supplier profiles, capability matrices, and market insights  RFI Model  Content: Company overview, product/service descriptions, certifications, references  Format: Questionnaire, capability matrix, open-ended response fields  Delivery: Supplier collaboration portal, email, or manual submission  Evaluation: Informal review by procurement, category managers  Operating Model  Portal-driven or manual distribution  Responses stored in supplier profiles or sourcing folders  No pricing or binding commitments  High-Level Process  Define RFI objectives and scope  Draft RFI template and questions  Identify target suppliers  Distribute RFI via portal or email  Collect and review responses  Document findings and inform next steps  Dynamics 365 Support  Supplier collaboration portal for RFI distribution and response intake  Vendor information management for storing RFI responses  Power BI for analyzing supplier capabilities

l3Processes.title: Identify continuous improvement opportunities
l3Processes.description:
The ongoing process of evaluating current production strategies, workflows, and
resource utilization to uncover areas where efficiency, cost control, quality,
or productivity can be enhanced. This involves analyzing existing operations,
soliciting feedback from stakeholders, reviewing production reports, and
benchmarking against best practices to pinpoint inefficiencies, bottlenecks, or
outdated procedures. The goal is to implement changes that drive measurable
improvements in operational performance, reduce manual data entry, increase
automation, and ensure the organization does not regress in its capabilities.

l3Processes.title: Develop procurement catalogs

apqc.description: Provide sourcing governance and perform category management

l3Processes.description:
Develop procurement catalogs establishes a governed, user-friendly buying experience by organizing approved goods and services into internal catalogs, connecting to supplier webstores via punchout, enabling vendor self‑service contributions, and importing supplier product lists to keep content current and compliant. Scope and intended outcomes
This process standardizes how employees discover items during requisitioning, ensures negotiated prices and terms are applied, shortens cycle time, and improves spend compliance. It spans catalog strategy and hierarchy design, internal catalog curation, external punchout enablement (cXML/OCI), vendor self‑service contributions through a portal, and scheduled imports to refresh products and prices. Core concepts to anchor the design  Procurement catalogs can expose stock and non‑stock items or category-only lines for services; a category hierarchy supports both itemized and non‑itemized purchasing, and is typically defined early in the ERP program so downstream requisition and PO flows align.  External (punchout) catalogs let requesters shop on a supplier’s site and return a validated cart to your requisition, commonly via cXML; this avoids duplicating large assortments while keeping approvals and accounting in your system of record.  Vendor self‑service (supplier collaboration portal) reduces buyer workload by letting vendors maintain item attributes, confirmations, and price lists within governance boundaries.  Vendor catalog import brings supplier items, pricing, and attributes into your internal catalogs using governed templates and mappings, ensuring searchability and price accuracy at scale.   Catalog types you’ll typically combine  Internal procurement catalogs: curated lists of items/services with categories, attributes, UOM, approved vendors, and trade/agreement pricing; ideal for frequently purchased goods and internal service menus.  External punchout catalogs: supplier-hosted assortments accessed from your requisitioning UI; appropriate when assortments change frequently or are too large to maintain internally.  Hybrid catalogs: internal for core SKUs plus punchout for long tails and specialized categories (e.g., MRO, IT accessories).   Governance decisions and data model
Define the procurement category hierarchy (depth, naming, coding), mandatory attributes, and approval policies by category. Establish catalog ownership (Category Manager, Buyer), SLAs for updates, and quality rules for item data (images, tax attributes, UNSPSC, substitutes). Align security so employees see only catalogs relevant to their role, region, cost center, and contract entitlements. 
Plan import formats and validations (e.g., cXML, CSV) and map supplier attributes to your canonical schema to support search facets and analytical rollups.  Integration and experience design  Requisition experience: employees browse catalogs filtered by policy; accounting is derived via category mapping and budgets can be checked before approval.  Punchout: configure the supplier connection, authentication, and return cart mapping; decide which categories route to punchout versus internal items.  Vendor self‑service: enable selected suppliers to propose items or maintain pricing via the collaboration portal under workflow and audit.   High - level process  Assess scope and policies: confirm which categories and suppliers will be catalog‑driven, which will use punchout, and which will remain free‑text or RFQ‑led; confirm approval thresholds and budget rules.  Design the catalog hierarchy: define category tree, attribute sets, and required data for items and services (including non‑itemized service categories) to support requisitioning and analytics.  Configure internal catalogs: create catalogs, assign products and categories, attach price/trade agreements, and set visibility/security for requester groups.  Enable punchout suppliers: establish external catalog connections (e.g., cXML), map categories to the supplier’s scope, and test round‑trip cart and accounting defaults.  Set up vendor self‑service: grant portal access, define what vendors can maintain (prices, lead times, substitutions), and route changes through workflow with audit.  Import vendor products: agree on file formats and cadence; validate content against your schema; load items and prices; link to catalogs and categories; log exceptions for supplier remediation.  Pilot and refine: run a pilot with key requesters and suppliers; measure findability, price coverage, and approval cycle time; adjust hierarchy, attributes, and visibility.  Launch with guided buying: publish catalogs, provide “how‑to” tips in the requisition workspace, and communicate when to use internal vs. punchout.  Operate and refresh: monitor price accuracy, broken links, and stale items; schedule vendor catalog imports; audit portal changes; retire duplicate or low‑use content.  Expand coverage: add categories and new punchout partners; introduce supplier-managed content for fast‑moving categories under tighter guardrails.   Roles and responsibilities  Procurement Category Manager: owns hierarchy, curation standards, and catalog roadmap; approves supplier‑submitted changes.  Buyer/Procurement Operations: configures catalogs, manages imports, resolves data exceptions, and monitors punchout uptime.  Supplier (via portal): maintains agreed product and price data; responds to content quality feedback; complies with update schedules.  Requester: follows catalog policies; uses approved catalogs or punchout; avoids free‑text when catalog coverage exists.   Dynamics 365 product support  Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management provides procurement category hierarchies, internal catalog setup, purchasing policies, and the supplier collaboration portal; it supports punchout catalogs and mapping of external carts back to requisitions. Vendor catalog import capabilities let you bring in supplier items and prices and attach them to your catalogs with governed templates.  Source‑to‑pay solution guidance highlights “Punch‑out catalogs” and “Vendor collaboration portal” in the standard capability map, confirming fit within the L2/L3 process model.   Operational controls and KPIs
Institute change workflows, reason codes, and periodic reviews; segregate duties between content authors and approvers. Track: catalog coverage (% of spend via catalogs), punchout success rate, search-to-add time, price variance vs. contract, stale item rate, and supplier content SLA adherence.

l3Processes.title: Develop supplier segments

apqc.description: Provide sourcing governance and perform category management

l3Processes.description:
Develop supplier segments organizes the vendor base into meaningful groups so procurement can target sourcing strategies, enforce policies, track supplier diversity, and optimize buying outcomes across categories, regions, and business units. This process supports both portal-driven segmentation (via supplier collaboration) and manual maintenance in the ERP, and it explicitly includes supplier diversity attributes and purchasing demographics. Scope and outcomes
The process defines the segmentation framework, enriches suppliers with the attributes that place them into segments, and keeps those segments current as suppliers onboard, update certifications, or performance shifts. Outcomes include clear segment definitions (e.g., strategic vs. transactional), validated diversity classifications, visibility into purchasing demographics (who buys from whom, where, and for what), and segment-specific playbooks for sourcing, risk, and performance management. Segmentation model (what to capture) Business identity: legal entity, parent/child relationships, tax IDs, NAICS/industry, geography. Commercial markers: category fit, price lists/trade agreements, contract status, capacity/lead times, delivery lanes. Risk and compliance: sanctions/KYC status, insurance, sustainability/ESG attestations, cybersecurity posture. Performance: on‑time delivery, quality metrics, responsiveness, dispute rate, claim history. Diversity: minority‑owned, women‑owned, veteran‑owned, disability‑owned, LGBTQ+‑owned, small/disadvantaged business, indigenous, local/regional designations; certification bodies; effective/expiry dates. Purchasing demographics: spend by business unit/region/cost center, category mix, order frequency, average PO value, tail vs. strategic spend share.  Data sources (how it gets populated) Supplier collaboration portal self‑service forms for profile, certifications, banking, sustainability and diversity disclosures, with evidence uploads and dates. Manual entry in system for vendor groups, classifications, category assignments, and policy flags when portal is not in scope. Controlled imports (CSV/cXML/API) from supplier registries (e.g., diversity certifiers), CLM systems, and performance data from receiving/quality modules.  Operating model Portal‑driven segmentation: suppliers submit/maintain profile and certifications; workflows validate and place suppliers into segments automatically based on rules. Manual in‑system segmentation: procurement analysts assign vendor groups, risk scores, diversity flags, and category memberships directly in ERP when collaboration is unavailable or during transition. Hybrid: start manual to establish baseline, then transition segments to portal upkeep; keep critical attributes under workflow (e.g., bank changes, diversity certification).  High‑level process Define segment taxonomy and rules Set the canonical list of segments (e.g., strategic, preferred, qualified, tail), diversity attributes, and risk tiers; publish the decision rules and data dictionary.   Configure master data and security Create vendor groups/classes, category associations, mandatory attributes, and approval workflows. Set role‑based access for who can view/change sensitive fields.   Enable the supplier collaboration portal Publish onboarding questionnaires, required documents, and self‑service profile pages for diversity, sustainability, and banking; map each field to ERP attributes.   Collect and validate data Intake portal submissions and imports; validate certifications (issuer, number, effective/expiry), run basic KYC/sanctions checks, and flag missing or expired evidence.   Apply segmentation rules Assign segment, risk tier, and diversity status based on rules (e.g., spend thresholds, category fit, performance, certifications). Record effective dates and rationale.   Enrich with purchasing demographics Link historic and rolling spend by BU/region/category to the supplier record; compute purchase frequency, order size, and tail‑spend indicators to refine segment placement.   Approve and publish segments Route changes through workflow for procurement/category manager approval; publish segments to sourcing, requisitioning policies, and supplier performance dashboards.   Govern usage in downstream processes Bind segments to policies (e.g., RFQ routing to “preferred,” auto‑blocks for “not qualified,” diversity‑spend targets in analytics, guided buying recommendations).   Monitor and refresh Schedule reviews for expiring certifications, material performance shifts, M&A events; automatically notify suppliers via portal to update expiring documents.   Improve the model Review segment drift vs. outcomes (savings, risk incidents, diversity‑spend attainment); refine rules and thresholds; retire unused attributes.    Roles and responsibilities Procurement/category manager: owns taxonomy, thresholds, and approvals; resolves exceptions. Supplier onboarding/operations: validates submissions, runs checks, and manages workflows. Diversity & inclusion lead (or ESG): defines accepted certification bodies and target reporting. Risk/compliance: maintains KYC/sanctions rules, approves sensitive changes. Finance/AP: verifies banking and tax data; ensures SoD and auditability. Business stakeholders: supply purchasing demographics and feedback on supplier fit.  Dynamics 365 support Vendor information management and vendor groups/classifications for segments, risk, and policies. Supplier collaboration portal for self‑service profile maintenance, confirmations, and document upload. Purchasing policies that reference vendor groups/segments to allow/deny categories or enforce RFQs. Trade agreements and approved vendor list to bind segments to price/contract usage. Analytics via Power BI/Microsoft Fabric to report diversity spend, segment performance, tail spend, and purchasing demographics.  Supplier diversity and purchasing demographics (practical guidance) Diversity capture: model diversity as structured fields with certification authority, certificate ID, effective/expiry, and attach artifacts; differentiate self‑attested vs. certified. Targets and tracking: define percentage goals (e.g., percent of addressable spend to certified diverse suppliers) by category and region; monitor progress monthly. Purchasing demographics: publish a quarterly heatmap showing who buys from which segments by business unit and location; use it to rebalance work to diverse/preferred suppliers without increasing risk. Guardrails: enforce evidence-on-file rules for diversity claims and auto‑expire designations upon certificate expiry; route reinstatement through validation workflow.  Operational controls and KPIs Controls: maker‑checker for profile changes; workflowed approvals for risk/diversity flags; automated reminders for expiring certifications; audit trails for attribute changes. KPIs: percent of spend through preferred/strategic segments; diverse‑supplier spend attainment; number of active diverse suppliers by category/region; average time to validate new supplier; share of tail spend; policy violations avoided; data completeness score for vendor profiles.  Deliverables Segmentation taxonomy and data dictionary. Configured vendor groups/classifications and workflows. Onboarding questionnaires and document lists (portal). Diversity certification playbook and accepted issuers list. Dashboards: diversity spend, purchasing demographics, segment performance.

apqc.description: Provide sourcing governance and perform category management

l3Processes.title: Define accounts payable policies

apqc.description: Provide sourcing governance and perform category management

l3Processes.description:
Define accounts payable policies establishes a single, governed rulebook for how your organization receives, validates, approves, pays, and accounts for supplier invoices—covering invoice matching, exceptions, cash discounts, payment methods, prepayments, rebates/allowances, and audit controls so AP is fast, compliant, and predictable. Scope and objectives Standardize AP across legal entities for invoicing, payments, cash discounting, prepayments, supplier rebates/allowances, and exceptions, with clear approval matrices and segregation of duties (SoD). Encode policies as system behavior (workflows, tolerances, holds, and validations) in Dynamics 365 Finance to reduce cycle time and errors.  Invoicing policies Invoice intake channels and requirements: define accepted channels (EDI/PEPPOL, vendor portal, email/OCR), mandatory header/line data, tax/VAT rules, and duplicate detection. In Dynamics 365 Finance, use invoice matching (two‑way/three‑way/totals) with variance tolerances; breaches route to workflow before posting. Matching and tolerances: set price/quantity/total thresholds by category or vendor; use three‑way matching where receiving is required; log and resolve discrepancies before posting. Non‑PO invoice policy: strictly limit categories that may bypass matching; enforce specialty approval and SoD; document required supporting evidence. Dispute management: define timelines and roles to resolve price/quantity defects and short‑shipments; hold invoices from payment until resolved.  Payment policies Payment run cadence and proposal rules: standardize weekly/biweekly runs using payment proposals (due‑date and cash‑discount date logic) with review/approval checkpoints. Methods of payment: permit ACH/wire/promissory notes; define positive pay and file formats; restrict checks and mandate treasury review for new methods/partners. Configure Positive Pay and payment types/fees in AP. Centralized payments and cross‑company: enable centralized AP where appropriate; set bank validation and reversal policies; log approvals for manual wires. Single Payment Requests (ad hoc): prohibit use when a PO is required; enforce privacy/SSPA and anti‑corruption controls; require controller approval for prepayments.  Cash discounting policies Terms and calculations: define standard terms, discount bases (tax included/excluded), and treatment of under/over‑payments; permit “outside window” discounts only under defined approvals. Configure “cash discount is calculated including tax” and cash‑discount administration settings. Payment selection: use payment proposals to prioritize invoices by discount date, then due date.  Prepayments and advances Distinguish prepayment invoices vs. prepayments; require controller approval, proof‑of‑execution after service, and recovery for undelivered goods—no “parking of funds.”   Supplier rebates and trade allowances Define eligibility, accrual method, required evidence, approval flow, and settlement cadence; align AP handling with procurement contracts/rebate programs.   Data governance, risk, and compliance Vendor master controls: workflow for vendor creation and bank changes, SSPA/privacy prerequisites, sanctions screening, and diversity classifications where required. SoD and auditability: dual control for bank edits and payments over threshold; versioned policy repository; retention and traceable approvals (MSApprovals).  KPIs and guardrails First‑pass match rate; invoice exception rate; average days to approve; on‑time payment rate; cash discount captured vs. lost; positive pay exceptions; duplicate‑invoice prevention rate; prepayment recovery rate.   High - level process
1) Define policy framework and decision rights (owners, SoD, approval matrices). Publish a versioned AP Policy Handbook and change process. 
2) Translate policies into system controls: configure matching methods/tolerances, required fields, workflows, and holds in AP.
3) Set payment governance: payment proposal criteria, payment methods, positive pay, centralized payments, and reversal rules. 
4) Establish cash‑discount rules and selection logic; align treasury calendars to capture discounts. 
5) Define prepayment controls and documentation (controller approvals, POE collection, recovery rules).
6) Codify non‑PO invoice exceptions and specialty categories; enforce workflows and documentation.
7) Implement vendor governance: onboarding, bank validation workflows, SSPA/privacy sanctions checks.
8) Roll out training, supplier guides (invoicing requirements, e‑invoice/EDI specs, positive pay remittance details).
9) Monitor and improve: track KPIs, audit exceptions, and refine thresholds; run quarterly policy reviews. Roles and responsibilities AP Manager: owns daily operations, exception queues, and KPI reporting; maintains matching and tolerance policies.  Controller: owns approval matrices, prepayment and non‑PO exception governance, and audit readiness.  Treasury: owns payment methods, bank relationships, positive pay, and wire approvals. Procurement: aligns invoice requirements to contracts, vendor terms, and rebates; ensures PO integrity and change‑management workflows. Risk/Compliance: monitors SoD, sanctions/privacy adherence, and policy enforcement.  Dynamics 365 configuration pointers Accounts payable home: vendor payment setup (methods, fees, terms); positive pay; payment proposals; centralized payments; reversals. Invoice matching: define totals/two‑way/three‑way matching with variance icons and tolerance thresholds; block posting outside tolerance. Payment proposals: select by due date or cash‑discount date; schedule and review prior to posting. Cash discounts: configure discount codes/series, tax‑inclusive calculation, and under/over‑payment handling.  Deliverables AP Policy Handbook (version‑controlled) and Approval/Signing Limit Matrix; Supplier Invoicing Guide; Payment Methods Playbook; Prepayment & POE Checklist; Matching & Tolerance Matrix; KPIs dashboard (Power BI).

l3Processes.title: Develop spend strategy

apqc.description: Provide sourcing governance and perform category management

l3Processes.description:
Develop spend strategy aligns procurement budgets to business goals, enforces budget control and signing limits, and governs in‑year budget transfers so sourcing and purchasing decisions stay compliant, timely, and cost‑effective. Scope and objectives
This process defines how procurement dollars are planned, approved, and steered across categories, business units, and regions. It codifies the rules for budget authoring and phasing, budget control checks during requisition and purchase order creation, monetary approval thresholds (signing limits), and the mechanics for budget transfers and amendments. Outcomes include predictable spend, higher budget adherence, fewer last‑minute waivers, and faster approvals without sacrificing control. Core policy levers  Budget ownership and structure: Define who owns category and cost‑center budgets; align to fiscal calendar, entities, and dimensions (business unit, region, category).  Spend guardrails: Set signing limits by role, risk, and category; require competitive sourcing or RFQ above thresholds; define change‑order time fences.  Control mode: Choose hard‑stop (block over‑budget) vs. soft‑warning (allow with justification) with consistent exception handling and audit.  Transfers and amendments: Permit controlled in‑year moves across cost centers/categories, with pre‑set caps, justification, and approvals.  Cash discounting and payment timing: Encourage early‑pay where economics are favorable and within cash/working‑capital targets.  Diversity and sustainability aims: Reserve portions of category budgets or set targets that influence award decisions without breaching policy.  Budgeting model (planning and phasing)
Start with a top‑down target by category and business unit, refine bottom‑up with forecast demand (projects, run‑rate, seasonality), and phase monthly or quarterly. Map contract commitments and blanket POs to budgets, and separate controllable OpEx vs. pass‑throughs. Maintain a controlled “contingency” slice for urgent/unplanned demand. Budget control at point of spend
Enable budget checks at requisition, purchase order confirmation, and change order. Controls should evaluate the correct accounting dimensions, recognize pre‑encumbrance/encumbrance where used, and consider open commitments. Define tolerances (for price variances, freight, taxes) and what triggers a re‑approval vs. warning. For centrally negotiated contracts, reserve capacity to reduce approval churn. Signing limits and approvals
Publish a single signing‑limit matrix that considers amount, risk (e.g., non‑standard terms), and category. Drive approvals from category and monetary thresholds, not individual preferences. Require dual approval for high‑risk items (e.g., prepayments, new vendor, non‑PO spend). Enforce Segregation of Duties so requesters cannot approve their own spend or vendor master/bank changes. Budget transfers and in‑year adjustments
Allow controlled transfers between cost centers/categories with:  Clear reasons (demand shift, price change, project acceleration).  Caps (e.g., ≤10% of original budget without CFO OK).  Traceable journal entries, effective dates, and audit trails.  A quarterly rebasing cadence to avoid constant micro‑moves.
Emergency top‑ups should be routed via a short, documented approval path with post‑facto review.  Change‑order and variance governance
Define which changes (quantity, price, schedule) require re‑approval and which can be auto‑accepted within tolerances. Log root causes (scope change, supplier lead time, FX) to continuously improve planning and supplier performance. Data model and analytics
Standardize dimensions for budget, commitment, and actuals (entity, BU, cost center, category, supplier). Provide spend dashboards for: budget vs. actuals vs. commitments, forecast‑to‑complete, contract utilization, early‑pay savings, and approval lead times. Track exceptions (over‑budget, rejections, waivers) and drill to transaction. High - level process  Define strategy and targets: Set fiscal targets by category/BU, phasing, reserve and contingency rules, and exception policy.  Allocate and publish budgets: Distribute to owners and load approved amounts with dimensions and phasing.  Configure controls and limits: Turn on budget control points, tolerances, signing limits, and workflows.  Execute with control: Run requisitions/POs against budgets; apply sourcing thresholds; use contracts/agreements to preserve rates and capacity.  Monitor performance: Review budget burn, commitments, and variances; surface at‑risk lines and approval bottlenecks.  Manage changes: Process budget transfers and amendments; enforce change‑order re‑approvals; document rationale.  Optimize for value: Capture early‑pay discounts, rebalance categories, and shift spend to preferred/diverse suppliers within policy.  Review and improve: Quarterly retrospective on policy hits, cycle time, and savings; adjust limits, tolerances, and targets.  Roles and responsibilities  Chief Procurement Officer: Owns policy, targets, and exception governance; approves large transfers and high‑risk waivers.  Category Manager: Builds category budgets, monitors burn and savings, drives rebalancing and supplier mix.  Finance Controller/FP&A: Sets fiscal guardrails, approves budget journals/transfers, ensures accounting integrity and audit trails.  Procurement Operations: Configures controls, runs approval workflows, and maintains signing‑limit matrices.  Budget Owners (BU/Cost Center): Plan bottom‑up needs, approve local spend within limits, request in‑year transfers.  Treasury/AP: Aligns payment timing and cash discount policies with working‑capital goals.  Dynamics 365 configuration pointers  Budgeting and Budget Control: Define budget models, codes, cycles, and budget control rules at the correct dimension set; enable checks at requisition/PO.  Procurement policies: Enforce catalog/category access, RFQ thresholds, and re‑approval rules on PO changes.  Signing limits and workflows: Implement monetary thresholds and conditional logic (category, risk, prepayment) for requisitions, agreements, POs, and change orders.  Budget transfers: Use budget register entries and transfer journals with workflow, attachments, and effective dates.  Analytics: Publish Power BI reports for budget vs. actuals vs. commitments, approval SLA, and exception management.  Operational controls and KPIs  Controls: Hard/soft budget checks at PR/PO, dual approval for high‑risk, SoD on vendor/bank updates, documented budget‑transfer journals, quarterly policy review.  KPIs: Budget adherence %, commitment coverage %, approval cycle time, waiver rate, % spend under contract, early‑pay savings realized, rework due to change‑order rejections, exception rate (over‑budget attempts), share to preferred/diverse suppliers within target.  Deliverables  Spend Strategy Playbook (targets, phasing, sourcing thresholds).  Signing‑Limit Matrix and Approval Routes.  Budget Control and Tolerance Settings (by category/entity).  Budget Transfer Policy and Journal Templates.  Quarterly Spend Review Pack (variance bridges, actions, owners, due dates).

l3Processes.title: Analyze sourcing market

apqc.description: Provide sourcing governance and perform category management

l3Processes.description:
Analyze sourcing market builds a repeatable market‑intelligence cadence to understand category dynamics, supplier landscape, and cost/risk drivers so you can choose the right sourcing strategy, timing, and partners. It connects internal demand and spend signals with external market data to inform RFx approach, negotiation levers, contract terms, and risk posture. Scope and objectives
Establish a disciplined view of the external market for each category, quantify price/capacity trends and competitive intensity, identify qualified and diverse suppliers, and translate insights into sourcing waves, award strategies, and guardrails for negotiations. Outcomes include better timing to market, improved price/term performance, risk‑adjusted supplier portfolios, and increased award share to diverse and sustainable suppliers. What to analyze  Market structure and capacity: supplier count/size, regional footprint, utilization, consolidation/M&A, entry barriers, alternate materials or make‑vs‑buy options.  Cost drivers and indices: commodities, labor, energy, logistics, FX, tariffs/duties; build should‑cost and index‑linked pricing constructs.  Competitive benchmarks: price corridors, service SLAs, lead times, warranty/after‑sales norms, rebate practices.  Risk signals: financial health, concentration, geo‑political exposure, ESG and regulatory compliance, cyber posture, supply chain resilience.  Diversity and sustainability: certified diverse suppliers, local/regional options, emissions and packaging standards, ethical sourcing.  Internal demand/spend: specification rationalization, aggregation opportunities, incumbent performance, quality/NCR history, total cost‑to‑serve.  High‑level process  Baseline demand and spend: consolidate 12–24 months of demand forecasts, specs, and contract expirations; segment by category, region, and criticality.  Map supplier landscape: identify incumbents, challengers, and new entrants; capture diversity and sustainability credentials; assess capacity and geographic coverage.  Quantify cost drivers: align categories to external indices and FX; produce should‑cost models with scenario bands (base/optimistic/pessimistic).  Assess risk and compliance: screen sanctions/SSPA/privacy, financial health, ESG attestations; rate suppliers and regions; define red‑flag thresholds.  Build the sourcing thesis: decide RFx vs. direct award, single vs. multi‑source, global vs. regional splits; set wave plan and award heuristics (price/quality/risk/ESG weights).  Define negotiation levers: index‑linked clauses, volume tiers, service credits, rebates, VMI/consignment, lead‑time commitments, dual‑sourcing safeguards.  Prepare the market: supplier briefings and data rooms via the collaboration portal; finalize RFx questionnaires and scoring; publish calendar and rules.  Execute RFx and analyze: run sealed bids/rounds; normalize price and non‑price terms against should‑cost; run sensitivity and multi‑objective scoring; produce award recommendation.  Close loop and monitor: codify index/FX adjustment rules, service KPIs, and business‑event alerts in contracts; schedule quarterly market refresh and supplier QBRs.  Roles and responsibilities  Category Manager: owns thesis, wave plan, RFx content, and award recommendations.  Sourcing Analyst: builds spend cuts, market models, should‑costs, and KPI dashboards.  Risk/Compliance: runs sanctions/SSPA/privacy/ESG checks; approves exceptions.  Finance/FP&A: validates index/FX mechanics and TCO; aligns to budgets and cash targets.  Sustainability & Diversity: curates certified suppliers and tracks award/spend attainment.  Legal/Contracts: authors indexation, SLA, and risk‑transfer clauses; ensures auditability.  Dynamics 365 and Microsoft support  Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management: procurement categories, RFQ management, trade agreements, vendor performance, rebate management, vendor consigned inventory, change management and approvals.  Supplier collaboration portal: supplier onboarding, profile updates, certifications, document exchange, bid responses.  Finance: AP terms alignment (cash discounts, payment methods), positive pay, centralized payments, rebate settlement.  Microsoft Fabric/Power BI: category spend cubes, price/index trackers, supplier scorecards, QBR packs, and variance bridges.  Copilot/Agents: narrative market summaries, anomaly detection on bid responses, next‑best negotiation levers, auto‑drafted RFx/Q&A and award memos.  Operational controls  Policy alignment: competitive bidding thresholds, AVL enforcement, RFx fairness rules, confidentiality, and SoD (RFx author vs. approver vs. awarder).  Data governance: versioned RFx, sealed bids, audit trails, index sources and update cadence, evidence for diversity certifications.  Risk gates: mandatory checks for sanctions/ESG/privacy; escalation paths for high‑risk awards; dual‑sourcing for critical items.  Contractization: index‑linked pricing clauses, service credits, exit/transition terms, step‑in rights, and business‑event alerts (late ASN, OTIF breaches).  KPIs and signals to track  Savings vs. should‑cost and vs. prior period; index‑adjusted price variance.  Award share to diverse and sustainable suppliers; number of certified diverse suppliers invited/responding/awarded.  Supplier risk score trend; dual‑source coverage for critical SKUs; regional exposure.  RFx cycle time; supplier participation and compliance rates; bid normalization deltas.  Contract leakage: off‑contract spend, price/term deviations, change‑order incidence.  Service KPIs: OTIF, lead‑time adherence, quality/NCR rate, corrective action closure.  Deliverables  Category market brief and should‑cost workbook with index mapping and scenarios.  Wave plan and negotiation playbook per category.  RFx package (questionnaire, scoring model, data room contents).  Award recommendation, TCO analysis, and risk register with mitigations.  Contract clause library for indexation, SLAs, rebates, and sustainability.  Quarterly market update pack and supplier QBR deck.  Implementation tips
Start with top 5 spend categories and near‑term expirations. Normalize specs to expand competition. Tie payment terms and early‑pay discounts to treasury targets. Use index bands rather than fixed prices for volatile inputs. Blend global and regional awards to hedge geopolitics and logistics. Bake supplier‑led innovation and sustainability metrics into SLAs—and pay for performance.

High‑level process Define segment taxonomy and rules Set the canonical list of segments (e.g., strategic, preferred, qualified, tail), diversity attributes, and risk tiers; publish the decision rules and data dictionary.   Configure master data and security Create vendor groups/classes, category associations, mandatory attributes, and approval workflows. Set role‑based access for who can view/change sensitive fields.   Enable the supplier collaboration portal Publish onboarding questionnaires, required documents, and self‑service profile pages for diversity, sustainability, and banking; map each field to ERP attributes.   Collect and validate data Intake portal submissions and imports; validate certifications (issuer, number, effective/expiry), run basic KYC/sanctions checks, and flag missing or expired evidence.   Apply segmentation rules Assign segment, risk tier, and diversity status based on rules (e.g., spend thresholds, category fit, performance, certifications). Record effective dates and rationale.   Enrich with purchasing demographics Link historic and rolling spend by BU/region/category to the supplier record; compute purchase frequency, order size, and tail‑spend indicators to refine segment placement.   Approve and publish segments Route changes through workflow for procurement/category manager approval; publish segments to sourcing, requisitioning policies, and supplier performance dashboards.   Govern usage in downstream processes Bind segments to policies (e.g., RFQ routing to “preferred,” auto‑blocks for “not qualified,” diversity‑spend targets in analytics, guided buying recommendations).   Monitor and refresh Schedule reviews for expiring certifications, material performance shifts, M&A events; automatically notify suppliers via portal to update expiring documents.   Improve the model Review segment drift vs. outcomes (savings, risk incidents, diversity‑spend attainment); refine rules and thresholds; retire unused attributes.    Roles and responsibilities Procurement/category manager: owns taxonomy, thresholds, and approvals; resolves exceptions. Supplier onboarding/operations: validates submissions, runs checks, and manages workflows. Diversity & inclusion lead (or ESG): defines accepted certification bodies and target reporting. Risk/compliance: maintains KYC/sanctions rules, approves sensitive changes. Finance/AP: verifies banking and tax data; ensures SoD and auditability. Business stakeholders: supply purchasing demographics and feedback on supplier fit.  Dynamics 365 support Vendor information management and vendor groups/classifications for segments, risk, and policies. Supplier collaboration portal for self‑service profile maintenance, confirmations, and document upload. Purchasing policies that reference vendor groups/segments to allow/deny categories or enforce RFQs. Trade agreements and approved vendor list to bind segments to price/contract usage. Analytics via Power BI/Microsoft Fabric to report diversity spend, segment performance, tail spend, and purchasing demographics.  Supplier diversity and purchasing demographics (practical guidance) Diversity capture: model diversity as structured fields with certification authority, certificate ID, effective/expiry, and attach artifacts; differentiate self‑attested vs. certified. Targets and tracking: define percentage goals (e.g., percent of addressable spend to certified diverse suppliers) by category and region; monitor progress monthly. Purchasing demographics: publish a quarterly heatmap showing who buys from which segments by business unit and location; use it to rebalance work to diverse/preferred suppliers without increasing risk. Guardrails: enforce evidence-on-file rules for diversity claims and auto‑expire designations upon certificate expiry; route reinstatement through validation workflow.  Operational controls and KPIs Controls: maker‑checker for profile changes; workflow approvals for risk/diversity flags; automated reminders for expiring certifications; audit trails for attribute changes. KPIs: percent of spend through preferred/strategic segments; diverse‑supplier spend attainment; number of active diverse suppliers by category/region; average time to validate new supplier; share of tail spend; policy violations avoided; data completeness score for vendor profiles.  Deliverables Segmentation taxonomy and data dictionary. Configured vendor groups/classifications and workflows. Onboarding questionnaires and document lists (portal). Diversity certification playbook and accepted issuers list. Dashboards: diversity spend, purchasing demographics, segment performance.

l3Processes.title: Onboard new suppliers
l3Processes.description:
This process governs the onboarding of new suppliers following successful application approval. It ensures vendors are properly qualified, set up in the system, and ready for transactional engagement. It includes account creation, contact and banking details setup, vendor demographic enrichment, legal entity replication, and qualification workflows. Scope and Outcomes  Converts approved applications into vendor accounts  Captures and validates supplier contact and banking details  Copies vendor profiles across legal entities  Maintains and updates vendor demographics  Qualifies suppliers for sourcing and purchasing  Outcomes include active vendor records, validated banking and tax data, enriched demographics, and qualified sourcing-ready suppliers  Onboarding Model Business Identity
Legal name, registration details, tax IDs, NAICS codes, geography, parent/child relationships Contact Information
Primary contacts, email, phone, address, role-based access Banking Details
Bank name, account number, routing code, currency, country, verification status Demographics
Industry, category fit, delivery lanes, capacity, lead times Compliance
Insurance, ESG attestations, cybersecurity posture, sanctions/KYC status Certifications
Diversity, quality, environmental credentials, effective/expiry dates Performance History
If applicable, includes legacy data on delivery, quality, responsiveness Legal Entity Replication
Copy vendor records to other legal entities with mapped attributes and controls Data Sources  Supplier collaboration portal: onboarding forms, document uploads, certifications  Manual entry: ERP-based vendor setup for non-portal suppliers  Controlled imports: CSV, cXML, API from registries, certifiers, CLM systems  Performance data: receiving, quality, and finance modules  Operating Model  Portal-driven onboarding: suppliers submit profiles, documents, certifications  Manual onboarding: procurement teams create vendor records directly in ERP  Hybrid: initial manual setup followed by portal-based updates and maintenance  High-Level Process Approve Application
Validate supplier application and convert to vendor record  Create Vendor Account
Generate vendor ID, assign vendor group, set up legal entity associations  Capture Contact and Banking Details
Enter and verify contact info, banking data, tax details  Enrich Vendor Profile
Add demographics, certifications, compliance attributes  Replicate Across Legal Entities
Copy vendor data to relevant entities with mapped fields and controls  Qualify Vendor
Assign category memberships, risk scores, diversity flags  Publish Vendor Record
Make vendor available for sourcing, RFQs, purchasing  Monitor and Maintain
Schedule reviews for expiring certifications, update profiles, notify suppliers   Roles and Responsibilities  Procurement/category manager: owns onboarding criteria, approvals, vendor group assignments  Supplier onboarding operations: validates submissions, manages workflows  Risk/compliance: maintains KYC/sanctions rules, approves sensitive changes  Diversity & inclusion lead: defines certification bodies, reporting targets  Finance/AP: verifies banking and tax data, ensures SoD and auditability  Business stakeholders: provide feedback on supplier fit and purchasing needs  Dynamics 365 Support  Vendor information management: vendor groups, classifications, segments  Supplier collaboration portal: self-service onboarding, document uploads  Workflow automation: approvals, validations, notifications  Purchasing policies: enforce sourcing rules, RFQ eligibility  Analytics: dashboards for onboarding throughput, diversity attainment, vendor performance  Deliverables  Configured vendor groups, classifications, workflows  Onboarding questionnaires, document lists  Diversity certification playbook, accepted issuers list  Dashboards: onboarding metrics, diversity spend, vendor performance

l3Processes.title: Manage active suppliers
l3Processes.description:
Manages the full lifecycle of active suppliers from onboarding through relationship termination. Includes maintaining vendor records, updating banking and contact details, managing electronic ordering channels, monitoring performance, enforcing compliance, and executing offboarding procedures. Scope & Outcomes
Ensures supplier data remains current and accurate 
Supports transactional readiness and compliance 
Enables collaboration and performance tracking 
Facilitates controlled supplier offboarding 
Outcomes include enriched vendor profiles, validated banking and tax data, active ordering channels, performance insights, and clean termination records   Supplier Management Model
Business Identity
Legal name, registration details, tax IDs, NAICS codes, geography, parent/child relationships Contacts & Access
Primary contacts, email, phone, address, role-based access, notification preferences Banking & Payment
Bank name, account number, routing code, currency, country, verification status, payment terms Demographics & Commercial Markers
Category fit, delivery lanes, capacity, lead times, pricing structures, trade agreements, contract status Compliance & Certifications
Insurance, ESG attestations, cybersecurity posture, sanctions/KYC status, diversity certifications, effective/expiry dates Performance
Delivery timeliness, quality metrics, responsiveness, dispute rate, claim history Ordering Channels
EDI/cXML setup, catalog integration, punchout configuration, approved vendor list bindings Lifecycle Status
Active, paused, terminated, reason codes, termination date, reinstatement eligibility Data Sources
Supplier collaboration portal: profile updates, document uploads, certifications 
ERP: vendor master updates, payment terms, ordering setup 
Controlled imports: CSV/cXML/API from supplier systems, CLM, performance modules 
Manual entry: vendor group changes, policy flags, offboarding triggers   Operating Model
Portal-driven updates: suppliers maintain profiles, submit certifications, request changes 
ERP maintenance: procurement teams manage sensitive fields, enforce workflows 
Hybrid: portal for routine updates, ERP for critical attributes and offboarding   High-Level Process  Maintain Vendor Profile
Update contact, banking, tax, and demographic data
Validate certifications and compliance attributes  Manage Ordering Channels
Configure EDI/cXML, catalogs, punchout setups
Bind vendors to approved lists and trade agreements  Monitor Performance & Compliance
Track delivery, quality, responsiveness
Flag issues, initiate corrective actions  Pause or Restrict Vendor
Apply status changes, restrict categories, enforce RFQ exclusions  Terminate Relationship
Record termination reason, date, supporting documents
Disable ordering channels, notify stakeholders  Review & Refresh
Schedule periodic reviews of vendor profiles, certifications, performance
Notify suppliers of expiring documents, request updates   Roles & Responsibilities
Procurement manager: owns vendor group assignments, ordering setup, offboarding approvals 
Supplier onboarding operations: validates updates, manages workflows 
Risk & compliance: maintains KYC/sanctions rules, approves sensitive changes 
Diversity & inclusion lead: monitors certification validity, reporting targets 
Finance/AP: verifies banking and tax data, ensures auditability 
Business stakeholders: provide feedback on supplier fit and performance   Dynamics 365 Support
Vendor information management: profile updates, banking, tax, compliance 
Supplier collaboration portal: self-service updates, document uploads 
Workflow automation: approvals, validations, notifications 
Purchasing policies: enforce sourcing rules, RFQ eligibility 
Analytics: dashboards for performance, diversity spend, lifecycle status   Deliverables  Updated vendor profiles with validated attributes  Active ordering channels and approved vendor lists  Performance dashboards and compliance reports  Offboarding records with audit trail  Diversity spend and lifecycle status metrics

l3Processes.title: Evaluate supplier performance
l3Processes.description:
Evaluates supplier performance across strategic and operational dimensions using defined KPIs, stakeholder feedback, and transactional data. Covers quality, delivery, pricing, compliance, and service metrics. Supports continuous improvement, risk mitigation, and sourcing decisions. Scope & Outcomes
Monitors supplier effectiveness post-onboarding through structured reviews and analytics. Identifies trends, anomalies, and non-conformances. Outcomes include performance dashboards, supplier scorecards, risk flags, and improvement plans. Performance Evaluation Model Delivery Metrics
% In Full, On Time (IFOT) Delivery lead time variance Missed delivery windows   Quality Metrics
Failed quality inspections Non-conformance reports Warranty claims / service issues   Pricing & Commercial Trends
Price variance analysis Unexpected cost increases Contract compliance   Forecast vs Actuals
Order forecast accuracy Volume adherence   Stakeholder Feedback
Internal surveys (procurement, operations) External surveys (supplier self-assessment)   Risk & Compliance
Recall incidents Sanctions / KYC status changes Insurance / ESG / cybersecurity posture   Service & Support
Responsiveness Dispute resolution rate Claim history    Data Sources ERP receiving and quality modules Supplier collaboration portal (self-assessments, service logs) CLM systems (contract compliance) Manual entry (non-conformance reports, survey results) Controlled imports (CSV, cXML, API from performance systems)  Operating Model Portal-driven performance updates and surveys ERP-based transactional data capture Hybrid model for manual and automated inputs  High-Level Process Define KPIs & Thresholds
Set performance metrics by category, region, business unit   Configure Data Capture
Enable ERP modules, portal forms, survey templates   Collect Performance Data
Intake from transactions, surveys, supplier inputs   Analyze & Score
Compute KPIs, identify anomalies, flag risks   Review & Validate
Procurement reviews scorecards, confirms issues   Publish Dashboards
Share performance insights with sourcing teams   Trigger Actions
Initiate improvement plans, pause sourcing, escalate issues   Monitor & Refresh
Schedule reviews, notify suppliers, update metrics    Roles & Responsibilities Procurement manager: owns KPI definitions, reviews performance, triggers actions Supplier onboarding / operations: manages portal inputs, validates data Risk / compliance: monitors sanctions, KYC, ESG, cybersecurity Diversity & inclusion: tracks diversity performance metrics Finance / AP: validates pricing, payment terms, cost anomalies Business stakeholders: provide feedback on supplier fit and service  Dynamics 365 Support Vendor information management: stores performance attributes Supplier collaboration portal: collects surveys, service logs Workflow automation: routes reviews, flags issues Purchasing policies: enforce sourcing restrictions based on performance Analytics (Power BI / Microsoft Fabric): visualize KPIs, trends, risk indicators  Deliverables Supplier scorecards Performance dashboards Non-conformance logs Survey results Recall reports Improvement plans Risk flags and escalation records

l3Processes.title: Manage supplier risk
l3Processes.description:
Manage Supplier Risk involves identifying, assessing, and mitigating risks across the supplier lifecycle. It includes evaluating suppliers during onboarding, monitoring ongoing risk factors, and enforcing compliance. The process is supported by both internal governance frameworks and Dynamics 365 SCM features. Scope & Outcomes Scope: Covers operational, financial, regulatory, privacy, societal, and geopolitical risks. Outcomes:Risk-tiered supplier profiles Alerts for high-risk vendors Risk dashboards and reports Compliance enforcement and audit trails Informed sourcing decisions    Risk Management Model Risk Dimensions  Operational: Contract types, subcontractor usage, evergreen agreements  Regulatory: Country corruption index, human rights, health & safety standards  Privacy: Data processing roles, volume, classification, security posture  Societal: Accessibility, sustainability, environmental impact  Financial: Supplier financial health, currency volatility, credit ratings  Security: Infosec posture, cybersecurity attestations, SCSAC assessments  Risk Assessment Tools  Supplier Risk Model: Calculates inherent and residual risk using internal and external data Supply Risk Assessment Workspace (D365 SCM):
Embedded Power BI reports  On-time in-full (OTIF) ratings  Risk scoring based on planned purchase orders  Forecasting future supply risk based on historical performance    Governance & Enforcement  Supplier Accountability Office (SAO): Manages violations, audits, and due diligence SGME (Supplier Governance, Monitoring and Enforcement): Tracks financial health, conflicts of interest, anti-corruption, and societal impact SCSAC (Supply Chain Security Assessment & Consulting): Provides security risk profiling and recommendations for third-party suppliers  Data Sources ERP & SCM: Supplier master data, transactional history, planned orders  Supplier Portal: Self-assessments, certifications, profile updates Power BI: Embedded dashboards for risk visualization  External Registries: Financial ratings, ESG scores, sanctions lists  Operating Model Portal-driven: Suppliers submit risk-related data and certifications  Automated Imports: Risk data from external sources and registries  Manual Oversight: Procurement and compliance teams validate and monitor  High-Level Process Define Risk Criteria: Set thresholds for each risk dimension  Configure Risk Model: Enable scoring logic and data sources  Collect Risk Data: Intake from portal, ERP, external feeds  Assess & Score: Compute risk tiers and flags  Review & Validate: Procurement and compliance teams review flagged suppliers  Publish Dashboards: Share risk insights with sourcing teams  Trigger Actions: Escalate, restrict, or offboard high-risk suppliers  Monitor & Refresh: Schedule reviews and update risk profiles  Roles & Responsibilities Procurement: Owns risk thresholds, reviews flagged suppliers  Compliance: Maintains KYC, sanctions, ESG rules  Supplier Onboarding: Validates submissions and manages workflows  Security Teams: Conduct SCSAC assessments and provide recommendations  Finance: Verifies financial health and auditability  Dynamics 365 Support SCM Risk Workspace: Central hub for supplier risk metrics and reports [3]  Supplier Risk Model: Built-in scoring engine for risk classification [2]  Supplier Portal: Self-service updates and certifications [7]  Power BI Integration: Visualize risk trends and supplier performance  Deliverables Risk taxonomy and scoring framework  Configured supplier risk model and dashboards  Supplier risk profiles and audit logs  Alerts and escalation workflows  SCSAC and SGME compliance reports

l3Processes.title: Manage supplier contracts
l3Processes.description:
Manages the lifecycle of supplier contracts from creation to renewal and termination. Supports contract authoring, review, approval, storage, and compliance tracking. Integrates Microsoft Syntex for intelligent document processing and Dynamics 365 SCM for purchase agreement workflows. Scope & Outcomes
Covers contract initiation, negotiation, execution, amendment, and renewal.
Outcomes include: Digitally stored, searchable contracts Validated purchase agreements Automated compliance checks Visibility into contract terms, obligations, and expiration timelines  Contract Management Model Contract Types  Master Service Agreements (MSA)  Statements of Work (SOW)  Purchase Agreements  Non-Disclosure Agreements (NDA)  Contract Attributes  Supplier name, legal entity, tax ID  Contract value, term, renewal clauses  Category alignment, delivery terms  Risk clauses, indemnity, liability limits  Diversity and ESG commitments  Microsoft Syntex Capabilities  AI-powered document classification and metadata extraction  Content understanding and compliance tagging  Integration with SharePoint and Teams for collaboration  Pay-as-you-go licensing with Azure Cost Management  Dynamics 365 SCM Capabilities  Purchase agreement workflows for supplier contracts  Integration with third-party CLM systems like Icertis Support for contract intelligence, clause libraries, and approval routing Contract status tracking and supplier performance linkage  Data Sources  Supplier collaboration portal: contract uploads, metadata entry  ERP: contract records, purchase agreement linkage  Syntex: document processing, classification, compliance tagging  CLM systems: contract lifecycle workflows, amendments  Manual entry: ad hoc contracts, legacy agreements  Operating Model  Portal-driven: suppliers submit contracts, metadata, amendments  Syntex automation: extract terms, classify documents, tag compliance  ERP integration: bind contracts to supplier records, enforce purchasing policies  Hybrid: manual review and automated processing for amendments and renewals  High-Level Process Initiate Contract
Define contract type, scope, and required clauses  Author & Review
Draft using templates, route for procurement and legal review  Approve & Sign
Use SAFE limits and authorized signatories per Microsoft policy  Store & Tag
Upload to SharePoint via Syntex, apply metadata and compliance tags  Link to ERP
Bind contract to supplier record and purchase agreement in D365 SCM  Monitor & Renew
Track expiration, renewal dates, compliance status  Amend & Extend
Submit PO amendment requests via MyOrder portal  Audit & Report
Generate dashboards for contract status, supplier performance, compliance   Roles & Responsibilities Procurement: owns contract templates, reviews, approvals  Legal (CELA): reviews legal terms, amendments Supplier Onboarding: validates submissions, manages workflows  Finance: verifies payment terms, auditability  Compliance: monitors risk clauses, ESG, diversity commitments  Dynamics 365 Support Vendor Information Management: stores contract metadata  Purchase Agreements: enforce sourcing rules, pricing terms  CLM Integration: supports Icertis and other providers Syntex: automates document processing, tagging, compliance  Deliverables Contract templates and clause libraries  Configured workflows for review, approval, storage  Syntex classification models and metadata schema  Purchase agreement records in D365 SCM  Dashboards for contract status, compliance, supplier performance

l3Processes.title: Manage supplier pricing
l3Processes.description: This process governs the setup, maintenance, and optimization of supplier pricing across the lifecycle—from onboarding through active engagement. It includes default pricing, manual and automated updates, integration with external systems, discounting mechanisms, vendor-specific pricing via trade agreements, vendor group pricing, and rebate programs. Scope & Outcomes Scope  Default and negotiated pricing  Manual and automated price updates  Integration with pricing APIs and external systems  Vendor-specific and group-based pricing  Rebate and incentive programs   Outcomes  Accurate, current pricing across vendors and categories  Enforced pricing policies and trade agreements  Visibility into discounts, rebates, and pricing trends  Optimized procurement spend and compliance  Pricing Management Model Default Pricing  Base price setup per item or service  Used when no trade agreement or discount applies   Manual & Automated Updates  Manual entry via ERP or supplier portal  Automated updates via APIs or integrations Effective date ranges, currency, and unit price tracking   Trade Agreements  Vendor-specific pricing rules  Quantity-based discounts, tiered pricing  Binding agreements linked to approved vendor lists   Vendor Groups  Shared pricing across vendor classifications  Category-level pricing enforcement   Rebate Programs  Structured rebate agreements configured in Dynamics 365 Eligibility criteria, approval workflows  Performance-based incentives and retrospective discounts   Discounting & Promotions  Negotiated discounts applied to unit price  Tracked via authenticated APIs and SCM workflows  Data Sources ERP: Vendor master data, trade agreements, pricing records  Supplier Portal: Self-service updates, rebate submissions  Pricing APIs: Real-time price retrieval, discount validation  CLM Systems: Contract-linked pricing terms  Rebate Modules: Incentive tracking and analysis   Operating Model Portal-driven: Suppliers maintain pricing profiles, submit rebate claims  ERP-integrated: Procurement teams manage trade agreements, pricing rules  Hybrid: Combines manual oversight with automated updates and validations  High-Level Process Define Pricing Strategy  Set default prices, discount tiers, rebate eligibility    Configure Pricing Records  Create trade agreements, vendor group pricing    Enable Supplier Portal  Publish pricing templates, rebate forms    Collect & Validate Pricing Data  Intake from portal, APIs, manual entry    Apply Pricing Rules  Enforce trade agreements, discount thresholds    Monitor & Adjust  Track price variance, rebate performance    Audit & Report  Generate spend analytics, rebate dashboards    Roles & Responsibilities Procurement: Owns pricing strategy, trade agreements  Finance/AP: Validates pricing, rebates, audit trails  Supplier Onboarding: Ensures pricing setup during onboarding  Business Stakeholders: Provide input on pricing fit and performance  Dynamics 365 Support Vendor Information Management: Pricing fields, trade agreements  Supplier Collaboration Portal: Price updates, rebate submissions  SCM: Purchase agreement workflows, pricing enforcement  Analytics: Power BI dashboards for pricing trends, rebate performance  Deliverables Configured pricing records and trade agreements  Rebate program setup and approval workflows  Pricing dashboards and spend analytics  Audit logs and compliance reports

l3Processes.title: Issue requests for quotation
l3Processes.parentL2: Source and contract goods and services
l3Processes.description: Solicits pricing and commercial terms from qualified suppliers for specific goods or services. RFQs are typically used for tactical sourcing and standardized purchases. Scope & Outcomes  Requests binding pricing and delivery terms  Compares supplier quotes  Outcomes include quote comparisons, approved vendor selections, and purchase agreement initiation  RFQ Model  Content: Item specifications, quantities, delivery terms, payment terms  Format: Structured form with line items and pricing fields  Delivery: Supplier portal, email, or integrated SCM workflows  Evaluation: Price comparison, compliance with terms, vendor eligibility  Operating Model  Portal-driven RFQ issuance  Responses linked to vendor records  May result in purchase agreement or PO creation  High-Level Process  Define RFQ scope and item specs  Select qualified suppliers  Issue RFQ via portal or SCM  Collect and validate quotes  Compare pricing and terms  Select supplier and initiate PO or agreement  Dynamics 365 Support  SCM purchase agreement workflows  Supplier collaboration portal for quote submission  Trade agreements and vendor group pricing enforcement  Analytics for quote comparison and spend tracking

l3Processes.title: Issue requests for proposals
l3Processes.parentL2: Source and contract goods and services
l3Processes.description: Used for complex sourcing needs requiring detailed proposals, including technical solutions, implementation plans, and pricing. RFPs support strategic procurement and long-term partnerships. Scope & Outcomes  Solicits comprehensive proposals  Evaluates technical, commercial, and strategic fit  Outcomes include proposal evaluations, supplier scoring, contract negotiation  RFP Model  Content: Scope of work, evaluation criteria, timelines, pricing structure  Format: Structured and narrative sections, attachments, compliance checklists  Delivery: Supplier portal or CLM system  Evaluation: Multi-criteria scoring, stakeholder review, negotiation  Operating Model  Portal-driven or CLM-integrated RFP issuance  Responses stored in sourcing folders  May result in contract award or shortlist for negotiation  High-Level Process  Define RFP scope and evaluation criteria  Draft RFP document and scoring rubric  Identify strategic suppliers  Issue RFP and manage Q&A  Collect proposals and score responses  Conduct negotiations and award contract  Dynamics 365 Support  Supplier collaboration portal for RFP distribution and intake  CLM integration for proposal review and contract workflows  Vendor information management for storing proposals  Power BI dashboards for scoring and evaluation

l3Processes.title: Evaluate supplier bids
l3Processes.parentL2: Source and contract goods and services
l3Processes.description:
Evaluates supplier responses to RFIs, RFQs, and RFPs using structured and unstructured methods. This process supports fair, transparent, and data-driven selection of vendors based on technical, commercial, and strategic criteria. It includes use of questionnaires, scorecards, site visits, and review of one-time vendors, write-in products, and unsolicited proposals. Scope & Outcomes  Applies evaluation frameworks to supplier bids  Supports scoring, validation, and stakeholder review  Outcomes include supplier rankings, bid scorecards, sourcing decisions, and documentation of rationale  Bid Evaluation Model Structured Inputs  Questionnaires and forms submitted via supplier portal  Standardized templates for technical, commercial, and compliance criteria  Scorecards with weighted metrics and scoring rubrics   Unstructured Inputs  Site visit reports  Stakeholder feedback  Write-in products on purchase requisitions  Product suggestions and unsolicited proposals   Vendor Types  Approved vendors  One-time vendors (used for niche or urgent needs)  Prospective vendors from RFIs or unsolicited proposals   Evaluation Criteria  Price competitiveness  Technical fit  Delivery capability  Compliance and certifications  Diversity and sustainability attributes  Risk profile and performance history    Operating Model Portal-driven: Suppliers submit bids, questionnaires, and documents  Manual review: Procurement and stakeholders assess site visits, write-ins, and proposals  Hybrid: Combines automated scoring with human judgment and qualitative review  High-Level Process Define Evaluation Framework
Set scoring criteria, weightings, and stakeholder roles  Collect Bid Responses
Intake via supplier portal, email, or manual entry  Score Structured Inputs
Apply scorecards to questionnaires and pricing sheets  Review Unstructured Inputs
Assess site visit findings, write-in products, and proposals  Rank Suppliers
Aggregate scores and feedback to generate rankings  Validate and Approve
Review by category managers and sourcing leads  Document Rationale
Record evaluation outcomes, scoring rationale, and sourcing decisions  Publish Results
